female-34-graves.name: Sarah Kennedy

missedConsequences.Hyperthyroidism: Untreated leads to atrial fibrillation, heart failure, osteoporosis, and thyroid storm.
missedConsequences.Thyroid Storm: Life-threatening emergency with mortality 10-30% even with treatment. Triggered by stress, infection, surgery.
missedConsequences.Atrial Fibrillation: Thyrotoxicosis is a common cause of new-onset AFib - must check TSH in all new AFib.

commonPitfalls: Attributing symptoms to anxiety or panic disorder without checking TSH
Missing thyroid storm - look for fever, AMS, and severe symptoms
Not checking for atrial fibrillation on ECG
Starting methimazole without checking pregnancy status (teratogenic)
Missing ophthalmopathy in Graves' disease
Not recognizing wide pulse pressure as sign of hyperthyroidism

mcqs.question: A 34-year-old woman presents with palpitations, weight loss, heat intolerance, and a diffusely enlarged thyroid. TSH is <0.01 and Free T4 is 4.8 (elevated). What is the BEST next step?

options.text: Start levothyroxine
options.explanation: Levothyroxine treats HYPOthyroidism. This patient is HYPERthyroid - it would make her worse!

options.text: Order thyroid ultrasound
options.explanation: Ultrasound can characterize nodules but doesn't help distinguish Graves' from other causes. TSI or RAIU is more specific.

options.text: Check TSI (thyroid stimulating immunoglobulin) or order radioactive iodine uptake
options.explanation: Need to determine etiology. TSI is specific for Graves'. RAIU differentiates Graves' (high uptake) from thyroiditis (low uptake). This guides treatment.

options.text: Immediate radioactive iodine ablation
options.explanation: RAI is a treatment option but requires confirming diagnosis and ruling out pregnancy first. Also need to control symptoms with medications before RAI.

mcqs.question: The patient is confirmed to have Graves' disease. Which medication should be started to control her symptoms while planning definitive therapy?

options.text: Propranolol
options.explanation: Beta-blockers (propranolol, atenolol) are first-line for symptom control - reduce heart rate, tremor, anxiety. Start immediately while planning definitive treatment.

options.text: Prednisone
options.explanation: Steroids are used for thyroid storm and severe ophthalmopathy, not routine hyperthyroidism.

options.text: Iodine solution (Lugol's)
options.explanation: Iodine can temporarily reduce thyroid hormone release but is only used before surgery or in thyroid storm, not as initial therapy.

options.text: Levothyroxine
options.explanation: This treats hypothyroidism, not hyperthyroidism. Would worsen the condition.

allDiagnoses.name: Graves' Disease
allDiagnoses.rationale: Classic presentation: young woman with diffuse goiter, ophthalmopathy risk, palpitations, weight loss, heat intolerance.

allDiagnoses.name: Hyperthyroidism
allDiagnoses.rationale: Symptoms classic for thyroid hormone excess: tachycardia, weight loss, heat intolerance, tremor.

allDiagnoses.name: Thyroid Storm
allDiagnoses.rationale: If fever >104°F, severe tachycardia, AMS, or cardiovascular collapse - this is thyroid storm, a medical emergency.

allDiagnoses.name: Atrial Fibrillation
allDiagnoses.rationale: Hyperthyroidism commonly causes AFib. Check ECG. May need rate control and anticoagulation.

allDiagnoses.name: Toxic Multinodular Goiter
allDiagnoses.rationale: Alternative cause of hyperthyroidism, especially in older patients. Nodular thyroid on exam.

allDiagnoses.name: Thyroiditis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Subacute or postpartum thyroiditis can cause transient hyperthyroidism. Usually self-limited.

allDiagnoses.name: Anxiety/Panic Disorder
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can mimic hyperthyroidism (palpitations, tremor, anxiety) but no weight loss, goiter, or eye findings.

allDiagnoses.name: Pheochromocytoma
allDiagnoses.rationale: Episodic hypertension, headache, sweating. Would not have goiter or low TSH.

female-34-graves.oldcartsNarrative: "This has been going on for about 3 months. My heart races all the time - I can feel it pounding even when I'm just sitting still. It's constant, not just sometimes. The pounding is maybe a 6 out of 10 in terms of how bothersome it is. Nothing really makes it better - it's there when I wake up and when I go to bed. I've also noticed I've lost weight even though I feel like I'm eating the same or more. I don't know exactly how much. I just don't feel like myself. I've been more anxious than usual and I'm not sleeping well. My hands seem a little shaky."

historyQuestions.text: When did you first start noticing these symptoms?
historyQuestions.answer: It's been going on for about 3 months now. The heart racing and weight loss started first, and it's been gradually getting worse.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Gradual onset over months is typical for hyperthyroidism; acute onset would suggest thyroiditis or thyroid storm

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about the weight loss
historyQuestions.answer: I've lost about 15 pounds in 3 months without trying. Actually, I've been eating more than usual - I'm hungry all the time. My appetite is great but I keep losing weight.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Weight loss despite increased appetite is classic for hyperthyroidism (hypermetabolic state)

historyQuestions.text: Are you sensitive to temperature?
historyQuestions.answer: I'm always hot! I've been turning the AC down to 65 and my husband is wearing sweaters. I'm sweating more than usual too.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Heat intolerance and sweating reflect increased metabolic rate

historyQuestions.text: Describe the heart racing
historyQuestions.answer: It's constant - I can feel my heart pounding even when I'm resting. Sometimes it feels irregular too. It's worse when I'm stressed but it never really goes away.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Palpitations are common. Irregularity suggests possible AFib - get ECG.

historyQuestions.text: Have you noticed any shakiness?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, my hands shake. It's embarrassing - people notice it. I can't hold a cup of coffee without spilling.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Fine tremor is characteristic of hyperthyroidism

historyQuestions.text: How is your mood?
historyQuestions.answer: I feel anxious and jittery all the time but there's no reason for it. I'm irritable with my family. I can't concentrate at work.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Anxiety, irritability, and difficulty concentrating are neuropsychiatric manifestations

historyQuestions.text: Any problems with your eyes?
historyQuestions.answer: They've been irritated and a little bulgy-looking. My husband said I look like I'm staring. They feel gritty.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Eye symptoms (proptosis, lid retraction, irritation) suggest Graves' ophthalmopathy - specific for Graves'

historyQuestions.text: Any changes in your periods?
historyQuestions.answer: They've been lighter and irregular. We've been trying to get pregnant for 6 months with no luck.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Menstrual irregularities common. Also important - need pregnancy test before treatment!

historyQuestions.text: Any family history of thyroid problems?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, my mom had thyroid problems - I think she had to take pills for it.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Graves' disease has genetic component - family history increases risk

historyQuestions.text: Have you noticed any neck swelling?
historyQuestions.answer: Actually, yes. My necklaces feel tighter and I can see a fullness in my neck when I swallow.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Goiter (thyroid enlargement) often present in Graves'

historyQuestions.text: How are you sleeping?
historyQuestions.answer: Terribly. I can't fall asleep, and when I do, I wake up multiple times. I feel wired all the time.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Insomnia and restlessness are common hyperthyroid symptoms

historyQuestions.text: Any changes in your bowel movements?
historyQuestions.answer: I've been going more often - maybe 3-4 times a day instead of once.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Increased GI motility leads to more frequent bowel movements in hyperthyroidism

historyQuestions.text: What medications do you take?
historyQuestions.answer: Just a multivitamin. I've been taking over-the-counter anxiety pills but they haven't helped.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Important to know current medications; OTC anxiety remedies won't help metabolic cause

historyQuestions.text: Any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: I'm allergic to sulfa - I got a rash from it.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Important before starting antithyroid medications

historyQuestions.text: Any other medical problems?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I've been healthy until all this started. No other medical problems.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Confirms previously healthy patient with new symptoms

historyQuestions.text: Is there any chance you could be pregnant?
historyQuestions.answer: I don't think so - my periods have been off and we've been trying for 6 months with no luck. But I haven't tested recently.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Critical - methimazole is teratogenic; must check pregnancy test before treatment

historyQuestions.text: Do you smoke?
historyQuestions.answer: I quit about 2 years ago. Used to smoke half a pack a day.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Smoking increases risk of Graves' ophthalmopathy and should be avoided

historyQuestions.text: What do you do for work?
historyQuestions.answer: I'm a graphic designer. I need my hands steady for my work, and the shaking has been making it really hard.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Occupation helps understand functional impact; tremor affecting work performance

historyQuestions.text: What worries you most about this?
historyQuestions.answer: I'm worried something is really wrong with me. My heart racing all the time is scary. And we really want to have a baby.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Addresses patient concerns; fertility is important consideration in treatment planning

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect planning.

physicalExam.finding: BP 148/72 (wide pulse pressure), HR 112 and regular, RR 18, Temp 99.8°F, SpO2 99%
physicalExam.reasoning: Tachycardia and wide pulse pressure (SBP-DBP = 76) classic for hyperthyroidism. Low-grade fever can occur.

physicalExam.finding: Thin, anxious-appearing woman. Restless, difficulty sitting still. Warm, moist skin. Fine tremor of outstretched hands.
physicalExam.reasoning: Classic hyperthyroid appearance: thin, anxious, diaphoretic, tremulous

physicalExam.finding: Mild bilateral proptosis. Lid lag present (sclera visible above iris on downgaze). Conjunctival injection. Lid retraction giving 'staring' appearance.
physicalExam.reasoning: Graves' ophthalmopathy: proptosis, lid lag, lid retraction are SPECIFIC for Graves' (not other hyperthyroid causes)

physicalExam.finding: Diffusely enlarged thyroid (approximately 2x normal), smooth, non-tender, no nodules. Thyroid bruit present on auscultation.
physicalExam.reasoning: Diffuse goiter with bruit = increased blood flow. Bruit highly suggestive of Graves'. Nodular goiter would suggest toxic MNG.

physicalExam.finding: Tachycardic, regular rhythm. Hyperdynamic precordium. Normal S1/S2, no murmurs. Bounding peripheral pulses.
physicalExam.reasoning: Hyperdynamic circulation from increased cardiac output. If irregular, consider AFib.

physicalExam.finding: Warm, moist, smooth skin. No pretibial myxedema. Palmar erythema present.
physicalExam.reasoning: Warm moist skin from vasodilation and sweating. Pretibial myxedema is specific for Graves' but less common.

physicalExam.finding: Fine tremor of outstretched hands. Hyperreflexia with brisk DTRs throughout. Normal strength.
physicalExam.reasoning: Tremor and hyperreflexia reflect sympathetic overactivity

labs.name: TSH
labs.reasoning: Suppressed TSH is the most sensitive screening test for hyperthyroidism. <0.01 indicates significant thyroid hormone excess.

labs.name: Free T4
labs.reasoning: Elevated Free T4 confirms thyroid hormone excess. Order with TSH to confirm hyperthyroidism.

labs.name: Free T3
labs.reasoning: Often elevated more than T4 in Graves'. Some patients have T3-toxicosis with normal T4.

labs.name: TSI (Thyroid Stimulating Immunoglobulin)
labs.reasoning: TSI is specific for Graves' disease. These antibodies stimulate the TSH receptor causing hyperthyroidism.

labs.name: Urine Pregnancy Test
labs.reasoning: MUST check before starting antithyroid drugs (methimazole is teratogenic in 1st trimester) or radioactive iodine (contraindicated in pregnancy).

labs.name: CBC
labs.reasoning: Baseline before starting methimazole (can cause agranulocytosis). Also rules out anemia.

labs.name: Liver Function Tests
labs.reasoning: Baseline before methimazole (can cause hepatotoxicity). Mild elevations can occur from hyperthyroidism itself.

imaging.name: ECG
imaging.result: Sinus tachycardia at 108 bpm. Normal axis. No ST changes. No AFib.
imaging.reasoning: Check for atrial fibrillation - occurs in 10-15% of hyperthyroid patients. This patient has sinus tachycardia.

imaging.name: Radioactive Iodine Uptake (RAIU)
imaging.result: Not done initially - TSI positive confirms Graves'. Would show elevated uptake (>35%) if done.
imaging.reasoning: RAIU helps distinguish Graves' (high uptake) from thyroiditis (low uptake) when diagnosis unclear. Not always needed if TSI positive.

imaging.name: Thyroid Ultrasound
imaging.result: Diffusely enlarged thyroid with increased vascularity. No nodules.
imaging.reasoning: Can confirm diffuse enlargement and rule out nodules. 'Thyroid inferno' on doppler suggests Graves'.

treatments.name: Propranolol
treatments.result: Propranolol 20-40mg TID for symptom control
treatments.reasoning: Beta-blockers provide rapid symptom relief (tachycardia, tremor, anxiety). Start immediately. Propranolol also blocks peripheral T4→T3 conversion.

treatments.name: Methimazole (antithyroid drug)
treatments.result: Methimazole 10-20mg daily
treatments.reasoning: Antithyroid drug blocks thyroid hormone synthesis. Takes 4-6 weeks for full effect. Preferred over PTU (except in 1st trimester pregnancy or thyroid storm).

treatments.name: Discuss definitive treatment options
treatments.result: Options: 1) Continue methimazole 12-18 months (30-50% remission), 2) Radioactive iodine ablation, 3) Thyroidectomy
treatments.reasoning: Antithyroid drugs may achieve remission. If not, or if patient prefers, RAI or surgery are definitive options.

consults.name: Endocrinology Consult
consults.result: Refer to endocrinology for management and discussion of definitive treatment options
consults.reasoning: Endocrine specialist helps guide treatment decisions, especially regarding RAI vs surgery vs medical management.

consults.name: Ophthalmology Referral
consults.result: Refer for formal evaluation of Graves' ophthalmopathy
consults.reasoning: Patient has eye symptoms. Ophthalmology can assess severity and guide treatment. Smoking cessation critical - worsens eye disease.

disposition.name: Outpatient management
disposition.result: Can be managed outpatient with close follow-up in 4-6 weeks
disposition.reasoning: Stable hyperthyroidism is managed outpatient. Thyroid storm would require hospitalization.

actualDiagnosis.name: Graves' Disease (Hyperthyroidism)
keyFindings: Classic symptoms: palpitations, weight loss despite increased appetite, heat intolerance, tremor, anxiety
Diffuse goiter with thyroid bruit (increased blood flow)
Graves' ophthalmopathy: proptosis, lid lag, lid retraction (specific for Graves')
Labs: Suppressed TSH (<0.01), elevated Free T4 (4.8), elevated Free T3
Wide pulse pressure (148/72 = 76 mmHg) - hyperdynamic circulation
Sinus tachycardia (no AFib on this presentation)
teachingPoints: Graves' disease is the most common cause of hyperthyroidism (60-80%). Autoimmune - TSI antibodies stimulate TSH receptor.
TSH is the best screening test. If low, check Free T4 and Free T3 to confirm hyperthyroidism.
Graves' ophthalmopathy (proptosis, lid lag, EOM dysfunction) is SPECIFIC for Graves' - doesn't occur in other hyperthyroid causes.
Other causes of hyperthyroidism: Toxic multinodular goiter, Toxic adenoma, Thyroiditis (subacute, postpartum), Iodine-induced, Factitious.
RAIU scan differentiates: High uptake = Graves' or toxic nodule. Low uptake = thyroiditis or exogenous thyroid hormone.
Treatment options: Antithyroid drugs (methimazole), Radioactive iodine ablation, Surgery. Beta-blockers for symptom control.
Methimazole side effects: Agranulocytosis (rare but serious - warn about fever/sore throat), hepatotoxicity, rash.
ALWAYS check pregnancy test before treatment. Methimazole teratogenic in 1st trimester. RAI contraindicated in pregnancy.
Thyroid storm: Fever >104°F, severe tachycardia, AMS, heart failure. Treat with PTU, iodine, beta-blockers, steroids, supportive care.
Wide pulse pressure occurs because thyroid hormone causes decreased SVR (vasodilation) and increased cardiac output.

guidelineReferences.name: ATA Guidelines for Hyperthyroidism and Thyrotoxicosis 2016
keyPoints: Methimazole preferred over PTU (except pregnancy 1st trimester, thyroid storm)
Beta-blockers for symptomatic relief in all patients
RAIU scan to differentiate hyperthyroidism causes
Treatment options: antithyroid drugs, RAI, surgery
Pregnancy test before RAI or methimazole

guidelineReferences.name: ATA/AACE Guidelines for Thyroid Storm Management
guidelineReferences.citation: American Thyroid Association / American Association of Clinical Endocrinology
keyPoints: Thyroid storm: Burch-Wartofsky score ≥45
PTU preferred in storm (blocks T4→T3 conversion)
Iodine given 1 hour AFTER PTU
Hydrocortisone for relative adrenal insufficiency
ICU admission and supportive care

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the patient had fever and altered mental status?
whatIfScenarios.description: Same hyperthyroidism but with temp 104.5°F, HR 160, agitation, and confusion

chiefComplaint.original: My heart races and I've lost weight even though I'm eating more than usual
chiefComplaint.modified: My heart is racing extremely fast, I have high fever, and I feel confused [patient agitated]

feedback.correct: Thyroid storm is a life-threatening emergency. PTU (blocks synthesis AND peripheral conversion), iodine (AFTER PTU), beta-blockers, steroids, cooling, ICU admission.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of storm. What's the specific medication sequence?
feedback.incorrect: Thyroid storm = ICU emergency. Treatment: (1) PTU (preferred over methimazole - also blocks T4→T3), (2) Iodine 1 hour AFTER PTU (blocks release), (3) Beta-blockers, (4) Steroids (block conversion), (5) Cooling, (6) Treat precipitant.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Stable hyperthyroidism = outpatient methimazole + beta-blocker. Thyroid storm = ICU, multi-drug regimen, high mortality if untreated. Look for precipitant (infection, surgery, iodine load).

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the patient was in atrial fibrillation?
whatIfScenarios.description: Hyperthyroidism with new irregular pulse at 138 bpm

chiefComplaint.original: My heart races and I've lost weight even though I'm eating more than usual
chiefComplaint.modified: My heart has been racing irregularly for 2 days and I feel short of breath

feedback.correct: AF is common in hyperthyroidism. Need anticoagulation (high stroke risk). Rate control harder - may need higher doses. AF often converts once euthyroid.
feedback.partial: Good link between thyroid and AF. What's special about anticoagulation here?
feedback.incorrect: Hyperthyroid AF: (1) Anticoagulate - high stroke risk regardless of CHA2DS2-VASc, (2) Rate control often requires higher beta-blocker doses, (3) Once euthyroid, many spontaneously convert to sinus rhythm. Address both problems.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: New-onset AF always check TSH - hyperthyroidism is a reversible cause. Treat the thyroid disease AND the AF. Anticoagulation often needed even in younger patients.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the patient was pregnant?
whatIfScenarios.description: Same presentation but patient is 10 weeks pregnant

chiefComplaint.original: My heart races and I've lost weight even though I'm eating more than usual
chiefComplaint.modified: My heart races and I've lost weight. I'm 10 weeks pregnant.

keyDiscriminators: Need to distinguish Graves' from gestational thyrotoxicosis

feedback.correct: Pregnant with hyperthyroidism: First distinguish gestational thyrotoxicosis (hCG-mediated, self-limited) from Graves'. If treatment needed in 1st trimester, use PTU (methimazole teratogenic). RAI absolutely contraindicated.
feedback.partial: Good concern for fetal effects. What medication changes in pregnancy?
feedback.incorrect: Pregnancy + hyperthyroidism: (1) Check TSI to differentiate Graves' from gestational thyrotoxicosis, (2) First trimester = PTU (methimazole causes aplasia cutis, choanal atresia), (3) Switch to methimazole in 2nd trimester (PTU hepatotoxicity), (4) RAI absolutely contraindicated.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Non-pregnant = methimazole preferred. Pregnant = PTU in 1st trimester, methimazole in 2nd/3rd. RAI contraindicated in pregnancy. Must also consider gestational thyrotoxicosis which resolves spontaneously.

female-52-primary.name: Charles Harris
female-52-primary.chiefComplaint: I've been exhausted for months and I keep feeling dizzy when I stand up

missedConsequences.Adrenal Crisis: Life-threatening hypotension, shock. Can be triggered by stress/illness. Give stress-dose steroids!
missedConsequences.Primary Adrenal Insufficiency: Without treatment, progressive weakness, hypotension, electrolyte abnormalities, death.

commonPitfalls: Missing the diagnosis - symptoms are vague (fatigue, weight loss, dizziness)
Not checking AM cortisol in unexplained hypotension or hyponatremia
Forgetting to give stress-dose steroids during illness/surgery
Not recognizing hyperpigmentation as clue to PRIMARY adrenal insufficiency
Missing the electrolyte pattern: hyponatremia + hyperkalemia

mcqs.question: A patient has fatigue, hypotension, hyponatremia (128), hyperkalemia (5.8), and hyperpigmentation. AM cortisol is 2.1 (low). What BEST distinguishes primary from secondary adrenal insufficiency?

options.text: Low cortisol level
options.explanation: Both primary and secondary have low cortisol.

options.text: Hyperpigmentation and hyperkalemia
options.explanation: PRIMARY: High ACTH causes hyperpigmentation (MSH cross-reactivity). Aldosterone deficiency causes hyperkalemia. SECONDARY: Low ACTH, no hyperpigmentation, normal potassium.

options.text: Hyponatremia
options.explanation: Hyponatremia occurs in both. Primary = aldosterone loss. Secondary = cortisol loss affects free water excretion.

options.text: Fatigue
options.explanation: Fatigue occurs in both primary and secondary.

mcqs.question: This patient develops fever and becomes hypotensive (BP 78/50) despite fluids. What is the IMMEDIATE treatment priority?

options.text: CT scan to find infection source
options.explanation: Imaging can wait. This is adrenal crisis - steroids are the priority.

options.text: IV hydrocortisone 100mg immediately
options.explanation: ADRENAL CRISIS is life-threatening. Give IV hydrocortisone immediately. Then fluids, find/treat trigger (infection).

options.text: Vasopressors first
options.explanation: Vasopressors won't work well without steroids in adrenal crisis. Give steroids first.

options.text: Wait for ACTH stimulation test results
options.explanation: Never wait for test results in crisis. Give steroids empirically, test later.

allDiagnoses.name: Primary Adrenal Insufficiency (Addison's)
allDiagnoses.rationale: Fatigue, hypotension, hyperpigmentation, hyponatremia + hyperkalemia. Classic.

allDiagnoses.name: Adrenal Crisis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can be precipitated by any stress. Hypotension, altered mental status = emergency.

allDiagnoses.name: Secondary Adrenal Insufficiency
allDiagnoses.rationale: Pituitary/hypothalamic problem. No hyperpigmentation, no hyperkalemia.

allDiagnoses.name: Hypothyroidism
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can cause fatigue but not this electrolyte pattern or hyperpigmentation.

allDiagnoses.name: Malignancy
allDiagnoses.rationale: Weight loss and fatigue, but doesn't explain hyperpigmentation or electrolytes.

female-52-primary.oldcartsNarrative: "I've been exhausted for about 6 months and it keeps getting worse. I'm dizzy when I stand up - I've nearly fainted a few times. I've lost about 20 pounds without trying - I'm just not hungry and food tastes different to me, like I crave salt. I've noticed my skin is darker, especially in my creases and gums, but I haven't been in the sun. I feel nauseated a lot. I have no energy to do anything. I don't take any medications. I have hypothyroidism that I thought was controlled."

historyQuestions.text: When did all this start?
historyQuestions.answer: It's been going on for about 6 months, maybe longer. It came on slowly - at first I thought I was just tired from working too much. But it's gotten progressively worse.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Gradual onset over months is typical for chronic primary adrenal insufficiency

historyQuestions.text: Describe the fatigue
historyQuestions.answer: It's been getting worse for 6 months. I used to be active but now I can barely get through the day. I have to rest constantly.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Progressive fatigue is hallmark of adrenal insufficiency - cortisol needed for energy.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about the dizziness
historyQuestions.answer: I get really lightheaded when I stand up. I've almost passed out several times. I have to sit on the edge of the bed before standing.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Orthostatic hypotension from volume depletion and cortisol deficiency.

historyQuestions.text: How much weight have you lost?
historyQuestions.answer: About 20 pounds over 6 months. I'm not trying to lose weight - I'm just not hungry and I feel nauseated.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Weight loss, anorexia, nausea are common in adrenal insufficiency.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about the skin changes
historyQuestions.answer: My skin has gotten darker even though I haven't been in the sun. It's especially dark in my palm creases, my elbows, and my gums look darker too.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Hyperpigmentation is SPECIFIC for PRIMARY adrenal insufficiency - high ACTH cross-reacts with MSH receptor.

historyQuestions.text: Any food cravings?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes! I crave salty foods like crazy. I put extra salt on everything. Pickles, chips, olives - I can't get enough.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Salt craving from aldosterone deficiency and sodium wasting.

historyQuestions.text: Any other medical conditions?
historyQuestions.answer: I have hypothyroidism. I also have vitiligo - patches of lighter skin. My mom had some autoimmune disease.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Autoimmune conditions cluster. Hashimoto's + vitiligo suggests autoimmune adrenal (polyglandular syndrome).

historyQuestions.text: What medications do you take?
historyQuestions.answer: Just levothyroxine for my thyroid. I haven't been on any steroids or anything.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Important to exclude steroid withdrawal as cause of secondary AI; thyroid replacement confirmed

historyQuestions.text: Any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Important before starting steroid therapy

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about the nausea
historyQuestions.answer: I feel nauseated most of the time, especially in the morning. Sometimes I vomit. Food just doesn't appeal to me.
historyQuestions.reasoning: GI symptoms (nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain) common in adrenal insufficiency

historyQuestions.text: Any food cravings?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes! I've been craving salt like crazy. I've been putting extra salt on everything and eating salty snacks constantly.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Salt craving is classic for primary AI - body trying to compensate for aldosterone deficiency and sodium loss

historyQuestions.text: Any autoimmune diseases in your family?
historyQuestions.answer: My mother had lupus. My sister has type 1 diabetes.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Family history of autoimmune disease supports autoimmune etiology (polyglandular syndrome)

historyQuestions.text: Have you been sick or had any stressful events recently?
historyQuestions.answer: I had a bad cold about 2 weeks ago. I felt even worse than usual during that time.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Illness/stress can precipitate adrenal crisis in patients with adrenal insufficiency

historyQuestions.text: Any prior surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: Just my appendix out as a teenager. Nothing on my adrenals or pituitary.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Rules out surgical cause of adrenal insufficiency

historyQuestions.text: What do you do for work?
historyQuestions.answer: I'm a teacher but I've had to take medical leave. I just don't have the energy to work.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Significant functional impairment from symptoms

historyQuestions.text: What worries you most?
historyQuestions.answer: I'm scared something is really wrong. I don't feel like myself at all. I just want to feel normal again.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Opens discussion about diagnosis and that treatment is very effective

physicalExam.finding: Supine BP 92/58 HR 98. Standing BP 74/50 HR 118. Significant orthostatic drop.
physicalExam.reasoning: Orthostatic hypotension from aldosterone deficiency (volume depletion) and cortisol deficiency.

physicalExam.finding: Thin, fatigued-appearing woman. Diffuse hyperpigmentation most notable in sun-exposed areas.
physicalExam.reasoning: Weight loss and hyperpigmentation are visible clues.

physicalExam.finding: Bronze hyperpigmentation of face, dorsal hands. Darker pigmentation in palmar creases, knuckles, elbows, areolae. Vitiligo patches on forearms.
physicalExam.reasoning: Hyperpigmentation in creases, pressure points, and mucous membranes is pathognomonic for primary AI.

physicalExam.finding: Hyperpigmented patches on buccal mucosa and gums.
physicalExam.reasoning: Mucosal hyperpigmentation is specific for primary adrenal insufficiency.

physicalExam.finding: Soft, non-tender. No hepatosplenomegaly.
physicalExam.reasoning: Non-specific exam. Rule out other pathology.

labs.name: BMP
labs.result: Na 128 (LOW), K 5.8 (HIGH), Cl 98, CO2 22, BUN 28, Cr 1.2, Glucose 68 (low)
labs.reasoning: Classic: Hyponatremia + Hyperkalemia. Low glucose. This pattern is highly suggestive of adrenal insufficiency.

labs.name: AM Cortisol
labs.result: 2.1 mcg/dL (LOW - normal >10, <3 is diagnostic of AI)
labs.reasoning: Low AM cortisol confirms adrenal insufficiency. <3 is essentially diagnostic.

labs.name: ACTH
labs.reasoning: HIGH ACTH = PRIMARY adrenal insufficiency (adrenal failure, pituitary trying to stimulate). Low ACTH = secondary.

labs.name: ACTH Stimulation Test
labs.result: Cortisol at 0 min: 2.1, at 60 min: 3.4 (failed to rise >18)
labs.reasoning: Confirms adrenal insufficiency. In primary AI, cortisol doesn't rise because adrenals are damaged.

labs.name: Aldosterone and Renin
labs.reasoning: Low aldosterone with high renin = primary adrenal insufficiency (adrenals can't make aldosterone).

labs.name: 21-Hydroxylase Antibodies
labs.reasoning: Confirms autoimmune etiology (Addison's disease). Most common cause in developed countries.

labs.name: TSH
labs.reasoning: May have concurrent autoimmune thyroiditis (polyglandular autoimmune syndrome).

imaging.name: CT Adrenals
imaging.result: Bilateral adrenal atrophy consistent with autoimmune adrenalitis.
imaging.reasoning: Shows small adrenals in autoimmune disease. Would show large adrenals in TB, hemorrhage, or metastatic disease.

treatments.name: Hydrocortisone (glucocorticoid replacement)
treatments.result: Hydrocortisone 15-20mg AM, 5-10mg afternoon
treatments.reasoning: Glucocorticoid replacement is lifesaving. Mimic diurnal pattern. Larger dose in morning.

treatments.name: Fludrocortisone (mineralocorticoid replacement)

actualDiagnosis.name: Primary Adrenal Insufficiency (Addison's Disease) - Autoimmune
keyFindings: Fatigue, weight loss, nausea, salt craving
Hyperpigmentation of creases, mucous membranes (specific for PRIMARY)
Low AM cortisol (2.1), failed ACTH stim test
teachingPoints: Adrenal insufficiency triad: Fatigue, hypotension, hyponatremia. Add hyperpigmentation and hyperkalemia for primary.
PRIMARY vs SECONDARY: Primary = adrenal failure (high ACTH, hyperpigmentation, hyperkalemia). Secondary = pituitary failure (low ACTH, no hyperpigmentation, normal K).
Hyperpigmentation: High ACTH stimulates melanocyte receptors (MSH cross-reactivity). Only in PRIMARY.
Electrolytes in PRIMARY: Hyponatremia (from aldosterone and cortisol deficiency) + Hyperkalemia (aldosterone deficiency).
Electrolytes in SECONDARY: Hyponatremia only. Aldosterone is preserved (RAAS intact).
Diagnosis: AM cortisol <3 is diagnostic. ACTH stim test confirms. ACTH level differentiates primary vs secondary.
Treatment PRIMARY: Hydrocortisone (glucocorticoid) + Fludrocortisone (mineralocorticoid).
Treatment SECONDARY: Hydrocortisone only (aldosterone is intact).
ADRENAL CRISIS: Life-threatening. Give IV hydrocortisone 100mg immediately. Don't wait for tests.
Sick day rules: Double steroids for illness, triple for severe stress/surgery. IM emergency kit. Medical alert bracelet.
Causes PRIMARY: Autoimmune (#1 in developed world), TB, adrenal hemorrhage, metastases, infections.
Causes SECONDARY: Pituitary tumor, pituitary surgery, CHRONIC STEROID USE (most common - suppresses ACTH).

guidelineReferences.name: Endocrine Society Guidelines for Adrenal Insufficiency 2016
guidelineReferences.citation: Bornstein SR et al. J Clin Endocrinol Metab. 2016;101(2):364-389
keyPoints: AM cortisol <3 μg/dL is diagnostic
ACTH stimulation test confirms diagnosis
High ACTH = primary; Low ACTH = secondary
Hydrocortisone 15-25 mg/day in divided doses
Fludrocortisone only needed in primary AI

guidelineReferences.name: Adrenal Crisis Guidelines - Endocrine Society
keyPoints: Adrenal crisis is life-threatening emergency
IV hydrocortisone 100mg immediately, then 200mg/24h
IV fluids for volume resuscitation
Do not delay treatment for diagnostic tests
Sick-day rules and emergency injection training essential

feedback.correct: ADRENAL CRISIS = IV hydrocortisone 100mg STAT. Don't wait for labs. Aggressive fluids with D5NS (treats hypoglycemia + hyponatremia). Find and treat precipitant (infection most common).
feedback.partial: Good recognition of adrenal crisis. What's the immediate treatment?
feedback.incorrect: Adrenal crisis is LIFE-THREATENING. Treatment: IV hydrocortisone 100mg immediately (don't wait for test results), aggressive IV fluids, dextrose if hypoglycemic, find/treat precipitating illness. Mortality is high if delayed.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Chronic adrenal insufficiency = outpatient management with oral steroids. Adrenal crisis = emergent IV steroids, resuscitation, ICU. The difference is hemodynamic instability.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the potassium was normal?
whatIfScenarios.description: Same presentation but K+ 4.2, no hyperpigmentation, low ACTH

chiefComplaint.original: I'm exhausted all the time and keep losing weight
chiefComplaint.modified: I'm exhausted all the time. I was on prednisone for my asthma for 6 months and stopped it a few weeks ago.

feedback.correct: Secondary AI: normal K+ (aldosterone intact), no hyperpigmentation (ACTH is LOW), low ACTH. Most commonly from chronic steroid use suppressing pituitary. Treatment = hydrocortisone only, no fludrocortisone needed.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of secondary AI. Why are the electrolytes different?
feedback.incorrect: Secondary AI = pituitary problem (low ACTH). Aldosterone is preserved (controlled by RAAS, not ACTH) so K+ is NORMAL. No hyperpigmentation (ACTH is low). Most common cause is exogenous steroids. Needs glucocorticoid replacement only.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Primary = adrenal destroyed, high ACTH, hyperK, hyperpigmentation, needs both hydrocortisone + fludrocortisone. Secondary = pituitary problem, low ACTH, normal K, no hyperpigmentation, needs hydrocortisone only.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the patient was going to surgery?
whatIfScenarios.description: Known adrenal insufficiency patient needing emergency appendectomy

chiefComplaint.original: I'm exhausted all the time and keep losing weight
chiefComplaint.modified: I have Addison's disease and I need emergency surgery for appendicitis

feedback.correct: AI patients need STRESS-DOSE STEROIDS for surgery. Major surgery: hydrocortisone 100mg IV before induction, then 50mg q8h for 24-72h, then taper to home dose. Prevents intraoperative adrenal crisis.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of need for stress dosing. What's the specific regimen?
feedback.incorrect: Patients with AI cannot mount cortisol response to stress. Major surgery needs: Hydrocortisone 100mg IV pre-op, then 50mg q8h x 24-72h, then rapid taper to home dose. Minor procedures: double home dose day of procedure.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Minor stress (dental work, minor illness) = double home dose. Moderate stress (significant illness, minor surgery) = 50mg hydrocortisone. Major stress (major surgery, sepsis, trauma) = 100mg + q8h dosing.

male-28-type1.variantDescription: Classic DKA in Type 1 diabetic with missed insulin

missedConsequences.Diabetic Ketoacidosis: Progressive acidosis leads to cardiac arrhythmias, cerebral edema, and death.
missedConsequences.Sepsis: Missed underlying infection worsens DKA and leads to septic shock.
missedConsequences.Acute Pancreatitis: Can both trigger and complicate DKA. Untreated leads to necrotizing pancreatitis.

commonPitfalls: Not giving insulin early enough - IV fluids alone won't correct DKA
Not replacing potassium - insulin shifts K+ intracellularly, causing dangerous hypokalemia
Stopping insulin when glucose normalizes - continue until anion gap closes
Not looking for precipitating cause (Infection, Infarction, Insulin missed, etc.)
Correcting glucose too quickly, increasing risk of cerebral edema

mcqs.question: Serum potassium is 5.8 mEq/L. ECG shows no significant changes. What is the correct approach?

options.text: Treat hyperkalemia urgently before starting DKA protocol
options.explanation: Insulin in DKA protocol will lower K+. Serum K+ is elevated due to acidosis but total body K+ is depleted.

options.text: Hold insulin until potassium is <5.5 mEq/L
options.explanation: Insulin should not be delayed - it will correct the hyperkalemia.

options.text: Start IV fluids and insulin; replace K+ once it falls below 5.3
options.explanation: Serum K+ is artificially elevated but total body K+ is depleted. As acidosis corrects, K+ will drop. Replace when <5.3.

options.text: This K+ indicates renal failure requiring modified protocol
options.explanation: Elevated K+ in DKA is from acidosis and insulin deficiency, not renal failure.

mcqs.question: After 4 hours, glucose is 210 mg/dL but pH is still 7.22 and anion gap is 20. What should be done?

options.text: Stop insulin drip since glucose is near target
options.explanation: NEVER stop insulin just because glucose normalized. DKA resolves when anion gap closes.

options.text: Continue insulin drip and add dextrose to IV fluids
options.explanation: Continue insulin until gap closes. Add D5/D10 when glucose <200-250 to prevent hypoglycemia while clearing ketones.

options.text: Switch to subcutaneous insulin since glucose is controlled
options.explanation: Too early - gap hasn't closed. Always overlap IV and SubQ insulin 1-2 hours when transitioning.

options.text: Increase insulin drip rate to close the gap faster
options.explanation: Standard rates are sufficient. Increasing too rapidly causes hypokalemia and hypoglycemia.

male-28-type1.oldcartsNarrative: "I've been throwing up since last night - probably 8 or 9 times. My stomach has been hurting all day, kind of all over but mostly in the middle. The pain is probably a 7 out of 10, constant and crampy. I also feel incredibly thirsty and I've been peeing a ton. I feel weak, dizzy, and kind of out of it. I'm a Type 1 diabetic - diagnosed when I was 14. I'm supposed to take Lantus at night and Humalog before meals, but I ran out of Lantus three days ago and couldn't afford to refill it. I checked my sugar last night and it was over 500. I don't drink much, don't smoke, don't use drugs."

historyQuestions.text: How much have you been drinking and urinating?

historyQuestions.text: Any chest pain or pressure?
historyQuestions.answer: No chest pain.
historyQuestions.reasoning: MI can precipitate DKA

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No, no allergies that I know of. I've been on insulin for years without problems.

historyQuestions.answer: No, just the insulin. I was taking metformin too but my doctor stopped it a while ago since my diabetes is Type 1.

historyQuestions.text: Any other medical problems besides diabetes?
historyQuestions.answer: No, just the Type 1 diabetes. I was diagnosed when I was 14. I've had DKA once before, about 3 years ago.

historyQuestions.text: Have you ever had any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries ever.
historyQuestions.reasoning: General health information

historyQuestions.answer: My grandmother had Type 2 diabetes. No one else with Type 1 though. They said mine is autoimmune.

historyQuestions.text: Do you smoke, drink alcohol, or use any drugs?
historyQuestions.answer: I don't smoke. I have a drink occasionally with friends, nothing heavy. No drugs.

historyQuestions.text: Who do you live with? Does anyone help you with your diabetes?

historyQuestions.reasoning: Social situation affects discharge planning and diabetes management support

physicalExam.finding: BP 102/68, HR 118, RR 26 (deep, Kussmaul pattern), Temp 99.1°F, SpO2 99% RA

physicalExam.finding: Ill-appearing young male, awake but lethargic. Dry mucous membranes, sunken eyes. Deep, rapid respirations. Fruity odor on breath.
physicalExam.reasoning: Classic DKA: dehydration, Kussmaul respirations, fruity (ketone) breath

physicalExam.finding: Diffusely tender to palpation, particularly epigastric. Soft, no rigidity or guarding. Hypoactive bowel sounds.

physicalExam.reasoning: Baseline cardiac exam

physicalExam.finding: Clear to auscultation. Deep, rapid respirations (Kussmaul pattern).
physicalExam.reasoning: Kussmaul respirations = respiratory compensation for metabolic acidosis

physicalExam.finding: Lethargic but arousable. Oriented to person and place, slow to answer. GCS 14 (E4V4M6).

labs.name: Point-of-care Glucose
labs.reasoning: Confirms severe hyperglycemia (DKA criteria: glucose >250)

labs.name: Basic Metabolic Panel
labs.result: Na 128 (corrected 136), K 5.8, Cl 92, CO2 10, BUN 32, Cr 1.6, Glucose 524
labs.reasoning: Low CO2 confirms metabolic acidosis. Elevated K+ from acidosis (total body depleted). Elevated BUN/Cr from dehydration. Calculate anion gap: 128-(92+10)=26 (elevated).

labs.name: Arterial Blood Gas
labs.reasoning: Confirms metabolic acidosis with respiratory compensation (low pCO2). pH <7.30 indicates severe DKA.

labs.name: Serum Beta-hydroxybutyrate
labs.reasoning: Confirms ketosis. Beta-hydroxybutyrate preferred over urine ketones.

labs.name: Complete Blood Count
labs.result: WBC 16.2 (stress response vs infection), Hgb 15.8 (hemoconcentration), Plt 245
labs.reasoning: Leukocytosis may be stress response or infection. Hemoconcentration from dehydration.

labs.name: Lipase
labs.reasoning: Mild elevation common in DKA. >3x normal would suggest pancreatitis.

labs.name: Urinalysis
labs.result: Glucose 4+, Ketones 3+, WBC 15-20, bacteria present, nitrite positive
labs.reasoning: UTI identified as precipitating cause! Must treat infection.

labs.name: Hemoglobin A1c
labs.reasoning: Indicates chronically poor control, supports medication adherence issues

imaging.name: Chest X-ray
imaging.result: No infiltrate or consolidation. Clear lungs.

imaging.name: ECG
imaging.result: Sinus tachycardia at 118. Normal intervals. No peaked T waves despite elevated K+. No ischemic changes.
imaging.reasoning: Monitor for hyperkalemia changes. Rule out ischemia.

treatments.name: IV Fluid Resuscitation - Normal Saline
treatments.result: 1L NS bolus over 1 hour, then 500mL/hr. Will transition to half-NS when glucose <200.
treatments.reasoning: CRITICAL first step - most DKA patients are 5-10L volume depleted

treatments.name: Regular Insulin Drip
treatments.result: 0.1 units/kg/hr IV infusion started after first liter of fluids and K+ confirmed >3.3
treatments.reasoning: Do NOT start insulin until K+ >3.3 and fluid resuscitation begun

treatments.name: Potassium Replacement

treatments.reasoning: CRITICAL - insulin drives K+ into cells. Hypokalemia is leading cause of death in DKA treatment.

treatments.name: Add Dextrose to IV fluids
treatments.result: Will add D5 to NS when glucose <200-250 to prevent hypoglycemia while continuing insulin
treatments.reasoning: Continue insulin until gap closes; dextrose prevents hypoglycemia

treatments.name: Antibiotics for UTI
treatments.result: Ceftriaxone 1g IV given for UTI as precipitating factor
treatments.reasoning: Must treat underlying infection

treatments.name: Sodium Bicarbonate
treatments.result: Consider only if pH <6.9 or life-threatening hyperkalemia
treatments.reasoning: Routine bicarb not recommended; may worsen intracellular acidosis and hypokalemia

monitoring.name: Strict I&O
monitoring.result: Foley placed for accurate output measurement
monitoring.reasoning: Monitor fluid balance in massive fluid shifts

disposition.name: Admit to ICU
disposition.result: Appropriate for severe DKA (pH <7.2, altered mental status)
disposition.reasoning: Severe DKA with pH 7.18 and altered mentation requires ICU monitoring

disposition.name: Admit to Medicine floor
disposition.result: Consider for mild-moderate DKA with pH >7.25
disposition.reasoning: This patient too sick for floor - needs ICU

disposition.name: Discharge home
disposition.reasoning: DKA requires admission for IV insulin and monitoring

actualDiagnosis.name: Diabetic Ketoacidosis (Severe) precipitated by UTI and Insulin Non-adherence
keyFindings: Type 1 diabetic with missed Lantus x3 days (insulin non-adherence)
teachingPoints: DKA criteria: Glucose >250, pH <7.3, HCO3 <18, Anion gap >12, Ketones positive
Severity: Mild pH 7.25-7.30, Moderate 7.00-7.24, Severe <7.00
Treatment priorities: 1) IV fluids (most are 5-10L depleted), 2) Potassium (if >3.3), 3) Insulin
Do NOT give insulin until K+ confirmed >3.3 - can cause fatal hypokalemia
Continue insulin until anion gap closes, NOT just until glucose normalizes
Add dextrose to fluids when glucose <200-250 to allow continued insulin
Always look for precipitating cause: Infection, Infarction, Insulin missed, Intoxication, Ischemia (bowel)
Transition to SubQ insulin: Give SubQ dose 1-2 hours BEFORE stopping IV insulin (need overlap)
Correct sodium for glucose: Add 1.6 mEq/L to Na for every 100 mg/dL glucose >100
Cerebral edema risk: avoid rapid glucose correction (target 50-70 mg/dL/hr decrease)

guidelineReferences.name: ADA Standards of Care: Hyperglycemic Crises 2024
keyPoints: DKA criteria: glucose >250, pH <7.3, HCO3 <18, AG >12, ketones+
Treatment: IV fluids, then K+ repletion, then insulin
Hold insulin if K+ <3.3 mEq/L
Continue insulin until AG closes, not just glucose normalizes
Search for precipitating cause (infection, MI, missed insulin)

guidelineReferences.name: ADA/AACE Consensus on DKA Management
keyPoints: IV fluids: NS 1-1.5L/hr initially
Insulin: 0.1-0.14 U/kg/hr continuous infusion
Add D5 when glucose <200-250 to continue insulin
Overlap SubQ and IV insulin by 1-2 hours
Monitor q1-2h until stable

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the initial potassium was 2.8?

chiefComplaint.original: I feel terrible - nausea, vomiting, and I can barely stay awake
chiefComplaint.modified: I feel terrible - nausea, vomiting, and I can barely stay awake. My muscles feel weak.

feedback.correct: Critical teaching point! K+ <3.3 = HOLD INSULIN until potassium repleted. Insulin drives K+ into cells and can cause fatal hypokalemia.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of low K+. What's the critical management change?
feedback.incorrect: NEVER give insulin when K+ <3.3 mEq/L. Insulin drives potassium into cells - with low starting K+, this causes fatal arrhythmias. Replete K+ first, then start insulin.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Normal/high K+ in DKA = start insulin immediately (K+ will fall as acidosis corrects). Low K+ = hold insulin, aggressively replete potassium, then start insulin. This is life-saving.

chiefComplaint.modified: I feel terrible - nausea, vomiting. I'm on an SGLT2 inhibitor for my diabetes.

keyDiscriminators: High anion gap with low glucose

feedback.correct: Excellent recognition! SGLT2 inhibitors (empagliflozin, canagliflozin, dapagliflozin) cause euglycemic DKA. Glucose can be <250 but patient is in DKA.
feedback.partial: Good awareness of euglycemic DKA. What medication class causes this?
feedback.incorrect: SGLT2 inhibitors cause EUGLYCEMIC DKA - glucose can be normal but patient has severe ketoacidosis. Easy to miss if only checking glucose. Always check pH and ketones in sick diabetics on SGLT2i.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Classic DKA = glucose >250. SGLT2 inhibitor-induced DKA = glucose can be normal/mildly elevated but has all other DKA features. Missed frequently because 'glucose isn't that high.' Always check anion gap and ketones.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if glucose was 900 with minimal acidosis?

chiefComplaint.original: I feel terrible - nausea, vomiting, and I can barely stay awake
chiefComplaint.modified: I'm so confused and thirsty. I've been urinating constantly for days.

feedback.correct: This is HHS, not DKA. Key differences: extreme hyperglycemia, hyperosmolality, minimal ketosis, more profound dehydration. Fluid resuscitation is even more critical.
feedback.partial: Good recognition this isn't classic DKA. What syndrome has extreme hyperglycemia with minimal ketosis?
feedback.incorrect: HHS (Hyperosmolar Hyperglycemic State) = glucose often >600, osmolality >320, minimal ketones, pH near-normal. More dehydration than DKA. Fluid resuscitation priority, insulin secondary.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: DKA = ketoacidosis with moderate hyperglycemia (often T1DM). HHS = extreme hyperglycemia and hyperosmolality without significant ketosis (often T2DM elderly). Both need fluids and insulin, but HHS patients are more dehydrated and prone to thrombosis.

female-38-dm2.chiefComplaint: I've been peeing a lot and I'm always thirsty
