missedConsequences.New Onset Type 2 Diabetes: Patient told to 'just drink less water' without labs. Progresses to DKA over next week, presents to ED obtunded with glucose 650, pH 7.1. ICU admission required.
missedConsequences.DKA/HHS: If diabetic ketoacidosis or hyperosmolar state were missed at presentation, could progress to coma and death.
missedConsequences.Type 1 Diabetes: Younger patients with DM can have Type 1. Missing this and not starting insulin can lead to rapid DKA.

commonPitfalls: Attributing polyuria to 'drinking too much water' without checking glucose - the thirst is CAUSED by hyperglycemia
Not recognizing the classic triad: polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss despite normal/increased appetite
Forgetting to check for DKA red flags: abdominal pain, vomiting, fruity breath, altered mental status
Missing the recurrent yeast infections as a clue to diabetes - glucose in urine feeds Candida
Not examining the skin - acanthosis nigricans is highly specific for insulin resistance
Forgetting family history - Type 2 DM has strong genetic component

mcqs.question: The patient has polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, and recurrent yeast infections. Which finding is MOST specific for diabetes?

options.text: Polyuria - excess urination always indicates diabetes
options.explanation: Polyuria has many causes including diabetes insipidus, psychogenic polydipsia, and medications.

options.text: Weight loss - diabetes always causes weight loss
options.explanation: Weight loss can have many causes and isn't specific to diabetes.

options.text: Recurrent vaginal yeast infections - glucose in tissues promotes Candida overgrowth
options.explanation: Recurrent candidal infections (vaginal yeast, thrush, skin) are highly suggestive of diabetes. Elevated glucose in secretions provides substrate for fungal growth.

options.text: Polydipsia - thirst only occurs with diabetes
options.explanation: Thirst can occur with dehydration, diabetes insipidus, and other conditions.

mcqs.question: You notice dark, velvety patches on the patient's neck and axillae. What does this finding indicate?

options.text: Poor hygiene - needs to wash better
options.explanation: This is NOT a hygiene issue. Acanthosis nigricans cannot be washed off.

options.text: Acanthosis nigricans - indicates insulin resistance
options.explanation: Acanthosis nigricans (velvety hyperpigmentation in skin folds) is a cutaneous marker of INSULIN RESISTANCE. It strongly suggests Type 2 DM or prediabetes.

options.text: Fungal infection requiring antifungal treatment
options.explanation: This is not a fungal infection. The appearance is distinctive - velvety, not scaly.

options.text: Normal skin variant, no clinical significance
options.explanation: Acanthosis nigricans is clinically significant and should prompt metabolic workup.

allDiagnoses.name: Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus
allDiagnoses.rationale: ⭐ ACTUAL DIAGNOSIS. Classic presentation: polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, fatigue, blurred vision, recurrent yeast infections. Age 38, overweight, family history, acanthosis.

allDiagnoses.name: Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can present at any age with similar symptoms. Usually more rapid onset, thinner patients, younger age. Requires C-peptide/antibodies to differentiate.

allDiagnoses.name: Prediabetes
allDiagnoses.rationale: With this degree of symptoms, full diabetes is more likely than prediabetes.

allDiagnoses.name: Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA)
allDiagnoses.rationale: Must exclude. Would expect N/V, abdominal pain, fruity breath, altered mental status, Kussmaul respirations.

allDiagnoses.name: Hyperosmolar Hyperglycemic State (HHS)
allDiagnoses.rationale: More common in Type 2 DM, elderly. Would expect extreme hyperglycemia (>600), altered mental status, severe dehydration.

allDiagnoses.name: Diabetes Insipidus
allDiagnoses.rationale: Causes polyuria and polydipsia but NO weight loss, NO yeast infections, and urine is dilute (not glucose-laden).

allDiagnoses.name: Psychogenic Polydipsia
allDiagnoses.rationale: Would expect psychiatric history. Doesn't explain weight loss, yeast infections, or elevated glucose.

allDiagnoses.name: Urinary Tract Infection
allDiagnoses.rationale: Would cause frequency/urgency but not polydipsia, weight loss, or systemic symptoms.

allDiagnoses.name: Hyperthyroidism
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can cause weight loss, tachycardia, but typically causes heat intolerance, tremor, not polyuria pattern.

female-38-dm2.oldcartsNarrative: "For about 2 months I've been urinating way more than usual - I have to go almost every hour during the day and I wake up 2-3 times at night to pee. I'm constantly thirsty, drinking water all the time, but it doesn't help. I've lost about 8 pounds without trying, which I thought was great until I realized something might be wrong. I'm exhausted all the time - not just tired, but like I have no energy. My vision has been getting blurry sometimes, especially at night. I've had three yeast infections in the past 3 months which is weird for me. Oh, and my feet have been tingling a little. My dad has diabetes and heart problems. I haven't really been to a doctor in a while - they said my blood pressure was 'borderline' last time but I didn't follow up."

historyQuestions.text: When did you start noticing these symptoms?
historyQuestions.answer: It's been going on for about 2 months. The frequent urination and thirst started first, then I noticed the weight loss and fatigue. It's all been gradually getting worse.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Gradual onset over months is typical for Type 2 DM; acute presentation would raise concern for DKA or Type 1

historyQuestions.text: Tell me more about the frequent urination.
historyQuestions.answer: I go to the bathroom almost once an hour during the day. At night I wake up 2-3 times to pee. It started about 2 months ago and has gotten worse.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Polyuria is cardinal symptom of DM. Osmotic diuresis from glucosuria causes frequent, large-volume urination.

historyQuestions.text: How much are you drinking?
historyQuestions.answer: I'm drinking water constantly - I keep a bottle at my desk and bedside. My mouth is always dry. Drinking helps for a few minutes then the thirst comes back.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Polydipsia is compensatory response to polyuria/dehydration. Classic diabetes symptom.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about the weight loss.
historyQuestions.answer: I've lost about 8 pounds in the last 2 months. I haven't been dieting or exercising more. Actually my appetite has been pretty normal, maybe even increased. My clothes are looser.
historyQuestions.reasoning: UNINTENTIONAL weight loss despite normal/increased appetite = cells can't use glucose, body breaks down fat and muscle. Red flag for diabetes.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about the yeast infections.
historyQuestions.answer: I've had three separate yeast infections in the past 3 months. I've only had one or two ever before. Each time I used over-the-counter cream and it helped, but they keep coming back.
historyQuestions.reasoning: RECURRENT CANDIDAL INFECTIONS are classic for undiagnosed diabetes - glucose in vaginal secretions promotes yeast overgrowth.

historyQuestions.text: Describe the vision changes.
historyQuestions.answer: Sometimes things look blurry, especially when I'm reading or at the end of the day. It comes and goes. I've never had vision problems before.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Blurred vision in diabetes is from osmotic changes in lens shape with fluctuating glucose levels.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about your father's diabetes.
historyQuestions.answer: My dad was diagnosed with diabetes in his 40s. He takes pills for it. He also has high blood pressure and had a heart attack a few years ago.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Type 2 DM has strong genetic component. First-degree relative with DM significantly increases risk.

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any abdominal pain, vomiting, or confusion?
historyQuestions.answer: No abdominal pain. No vomiting. I feel tired but not confused. My breathing is normal.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Screening for DKA red flags - abdominal pain, N/V, fruity breath, altered mental status, Kussmaul breathing. Absent here = reassuring.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about the tingling in your feet.
historyQuestions.answer: For about 3 weeks I've noticed a mild tingling in both feet, especially at the end of the day. It's not painful, just kind of distracting.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Early diabetic peripheral neuropathy. Suggests hyperglycemia has been present long enough to cause nerve damage.

historyQuestions.text: What medications do you take?
historyQuestions.answer: Nothing prescribed. I take some vitamins occasionally. They told me my blood pressure was borderline but I never started anything.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No current medications; borderline hypertension fits metabolic syndrome pattern

historyQuestions.text: Any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Important before prescribing diabetes medications

historyQuestions.text: Any other medical problems?
historyQuestions.answer: Nothing diagnosed. They told me my blood pressure was 'borderline' and my cholesterol was a little high last time I was checked, but I never followed up.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Borderline BP and high cholesterol fit metabolic syndrome; Type 2 DM is part of this cluster

historyQuestions.text: Any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: I had my appendix out when I was 15. Nothing else.
historyQuestions.reasoning: General surgical history

historyQuestions.text: Do you smoke or drink alcohol?
historyQuestions.answer: I don't smoke. I have a glass of wine occasionally, maybe once or twice a week.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Social history; alcohol intake affects diabetes management and calorie intake

historyQuestions.text: What do you do for work?
historyQuestions.answer: I'm an office manager. Desk job, sitting most of the day. Long hours, pretty stressful.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Sedentary occupation contributes to obesity and diabetes risk

historyQuestions.text: What worries you most about this?
historyQuestions.answer: I'm scared I have diabetes like my dad. He has to take shots now and I really don't want to do that. Will this change my whole life?
historyQuestions.reasoning: Addresses patient fears; many Type 2 can be managed with pills and lifestyle initially

diet_eating_patterns.answer: I eat out a lot. Fast food, takeout. Don't have time to cook with my schedule.
diet_eating_patterns.relevance: High processed food intake contributes to obesity and diabetes risk. Key modifiable factor for management.

diet_sugary_beverages.answer: I drink a lot of soda. Probably 3-4 cans a day. And sweetened iced tea.
diet_sugary_beverages.relevance: Sugar-sweetened beverage consumption is major diabetes risk factor. Important target for intervention.

occupation_current_job.answer: Office manager. I sit at a desk all day. Don't get much movement.
occupation_current_job.relevance: Sedentary occupation contributes to metabolic syndrome and diabetes risk.

exercise_activity_level.answer: I don't exercise. By the time I get home I'm too tired.
exercise_activity_level.relevance: Physical inactivity is major modifiable risk factor for type 2 diabetes.

social_work_schedule.answer: Long hours. Stressful. I'm the only one supporting my family right now.
social_work_schedule.relevance: Work stress and long hours limit time for healthy eating and exercise; chronic stress affects glucose metabolism.

housing_food_environment.answer: Not a lot of healthy options near my work. Mostly fast food and convenience stores.
housing_food_environment.relevance: Food desert/unhealthy food environment is social determinant of diabetes risk.

social_family_history_detailed.answer: Mom has diabetes, so does her sister. Dad died of a heart attack at 55.
social_family_history_detailed.relevance: Strong family history of diabetes and cardiovascular disease increases risk substantially.

social_healthcare_screening.answer: I haven't been to a doctor in years. No time, and it costs money.
social_healthcare_screening.relevance: Lack of preventive care means prediabetes was never caught and addressed.

physicalExam.finding: BP 102/68 (slightly low), HR 104 (mild tachycardia), RR 18 (normal, no Kussmaul), Temp 98.6°F, SpO2 99%.
physicalExam.reasoning: Tachycardia may reflect dehydration. Normal respirations argue against DKA.

physicalExam.finding: 38-year-old woman appearing mildly fatigued. Slightly overweight (BMI 28). Dry lips and oral mucosa.
physicalExam.reasoning: Dry mucous membranes from dehydration. Overweight is risk factor for Type 2 DM.

physicalExam.finding: ACANTHOSIS NIGRICANS present - dark, velvety, hyperpigmented patches on posterior neck and in axillae. Cannot be wiped off.
physicalExam.reasoning: ACANTHOSIS NIGRICANS is a marker of INSULIN RESISTANCE. Highly specific finding for Type 2 DM/metabolic syndrome.

physicalExam.finding: Unable to fully visualize fundus due to undilated exam. No obvious hemorrhages or exudates visible.
physicalExam.reasoning: Ideally would dilate to assess for diabetic retinopathy, but new diagnosis warrants ophthalmology referral anyway.

physicalExam.finding: Regular rate and rhythm, tachycardic at 104. No murmurs. No JVD. Peripheral pulses intact.
physicalExam.reasoning: Assess for cardiovascular complications. Tachycardia likely from dehydration.

physicalExam.finding: Skin intact, no ulcers. Mild decreased sensation to monofilament testing bilaterally in stocking distribution. Pulses palpable.
physicalExam.reasoning: Early peripheral neuropathy confirmed. Foot exam is critical in diabetes - assessing for neuropathy and vascular disease.

physicalExam.finding: Erythematous, mildly macerated patch under left breast with satellite pustules - consistent with candidal intertrigo.
physicalExam.reasoning: Another candidal infection - skin fold infections common in diabetes due to glucose in sweat/skin.

labs.name: Hemoglobin A1c
labs.result: HbA1c: 11.2% (markedly elevated, indicating chronic hyperglycemia over past 2-3 months)
labs.reasoning: Confirms chronic hyperglycemia. >6.5% diagnostic of diabetes. 11.2% indicates poor control.

labs.name: Basic Metabolic Panel
labs.result: Na 131, K 4.8, Cl 98, CO2 24, BUN 22, Cr 1.1, Glucose 342. Anion gap 9.
labs.reasoning: Mild hyponatremia from hyperglycemia. Normal anion gap rules out DKA. Assess renal function.

labs.name: Urinalysis
labs.result: Glucose 3+, ketones trace, no WBC/RBC/bacteria. Specific gravity 1.025.
labs.reasoning: Glucosuria confirms renal threshold exceeded. Trace ketones but no significant ketonuria.

labs.name: Lipid Panel
labs.reasoning: Dyslipidemia common in metabolic syndrome. Guides statin therapy decision.

labs.name: Comprehensive Metabolic Panel
labs.result: AST 42, ALT 58, Alk Phos 88. Albumin 4.0. Otherwise as BMP.
labs.reasoning: Mild transaminitis - check for NAFLD. Important before starting metformin.

labs.name: Urine Albumin/Creatinine Ratio
labs.result: UACR: 45 mg/g (mildly elevated, indicating early diabetic nephropathy)
labs.reasoning: Screens for diabetic nephropathy. >30 mg/g indicates microalbuminuria.

imaging.name: No imaging indicated
imaging.result: No acute imaging needed for uncomplicated new-onset Type 2 DM.
imaging.reasoning: Imaging not routinely indicated unless complications suspected.

treatments.name: Start Metformin 500mg BID
treatments.result: Metformin initiated. Patient counseled on GI side effects, to take with food, and to titrate up.
treatments.reasoning: First-line medication for Type 2 DM. Safe, effective, evidence-based.

treatments.name: Encourage oral hydration
treatments.result: Patient instructed to drink water liberally today.
treatments.reasoning: Mild dehydration from osmotic diuresis. Oral rehydration appropriate if tolerating PO.

consults.name: Endocrinology referral
consults.result: Outpatient endocrinology appointment scheduled for next week.
consults.reasoning: Not urgent but helpful for comprehensive diabetes management planning.

consults.name: Diabetes education
consults.result: Diabetes educator visit scheduled. Will cover glucose monitoring, diet, medication, sick day rules.
consults.reasoning: Education is critical for self-management of diabetes.

consults.name: Ophthalmology referral
consults.result: Dilated eye exam scheduled to screen for diabetic retinopathy.
consults.reasoning: Baseline eye exam indicated for all new diabetes diagnoses.

disposition.name: Discharge with close follow-up
disposition.result: Patient stable, no DKA. Discharged with metformin, glucometer, follow-up in 1 week.
disposition.reasoning: Appropriate for stable new Type 2 DM without DKA/HHS.

actualDiagnosis.name: New Onset Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus
keyFindings: Unintentional weight loss (8 lbs) despite normal appetite
Recurrent vaginal yeast infections (3 in 3 months)
ACANTHOSIS NIGRICANS on exam (insulin resistance marker)
No DKA: normal CO2, no acidosis, normal mental status
teachingPoints: THE 3 P's: Polyuria, Polydipsia, and unintentional weight loss (Polyphagia less common) are classic for diabetes.
RECURRENT INFECTIONS: Yeast infections, skin infections, and UTIs should prompt glucose check - glucose feeds pathogens.
ACANTHOSIS NIGRICANS: Velvety hyperpigmentation in skin folds = insulin resistance. Highly specific sign.
A1c FOR DIAGNOSIS: HbA1c ≥6.5% is diagnostic of diabetes. Also tells you about glucose control over past 3 months.
ALWAYS RULE OUT DKA: Even in Type 2 DM. Check for N/V, abdominal pain, fruity breath, altered mental status, Kussmaul respirations.
METFORMIN IS FIRST-LINE: Safe, effective, reduces cardiovascular risk, no hypoglycemia risk, inexpensive.
COMPLICATIONS SCREENING: At diagnosis, screen for retinopathy (eye exam), nephropathy (urine albumin), neuropathy (foot exam), cardiovascular disease.

guidelineReferences.name: ADA Standards of Care in Diabetes 2024
keyPoints: Diagnosis: FPG ≥126, 2h OGTT ≥200, A1c ≥6.5%, or random glucose ≥200 with symptoms
A1c target <7% for most adults; individualize based on patient factors
Metformin is first-line pharmacotherapy
Add GLP-1 RA or SGLT2i for cardiovascular or renal benefit
Comprehensive complication screening at diagnosis and annually

guidelineReferences.name: ADA/EASD Consensus on Type 2 DM Management 2022
guidelineReferences.citation: Davies MJ et al. Diabetes Care. 2022;45(11):2753-2786
keyPoints: Person-centered approach to glycemic management
Weight management is foundational
GLP-1 RA preferred if established ASCVD or high CV risk
SGLT2i for heart failure or CKD regardless of A1c
Combination therapy if A1c not at goal on metformin

closureScript.diagnosis: Ms. Edwards, I have important news. Your blood tests show that you have diabetes. Your blood sugar is quite high at 324, and your A1c test shows it's been elevated for a while. This explains all your symptoms - the frequent urination, the thirst, the weight loss, the yeast infections, and the blurry vision are all caused by high blood sugar.
closureScript.plan: The good news is this is very treatable. I'm starting you on a medication called metformin that will help your body use insulin better and lower your blood sugar. You'll need to check your blood sugar at home - we'll give you a glucometer today. I'm also setting you up with our diabetes educator who will teach you about diet, monitoring, and managing this condition. And you'll need an eye exam to make sure your eyes are okay.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: Come back right away if you develop severe nausea, vomiting, or abdominal pain, if you feel confused or very drowsy, or if you have trouble breathing. These could be signs your blood sugar is dangerously high.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: I know a diabetes diagnosis can feel overwhelming. But with good management, people with diabetes live full, healthy lives. Your dad has lived with diabetes for years. The key is taking it seriously, taking your medication, and making some lifestyle changes. You caught this relatively early, which is actually good news.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the patient had nausea, vomiting, and fruity breath?
whatIfScenarios.description: Same hyperglycemia but with Kussmaul respirations, fruity breath, and altered mental status

chiefComplaint.original: I've been so thirsty and peeing all the time
chiefComplaint.modified: I've been vomiting all day, my stomach hurts, and I feel really confused

feedback.correct: DKA = hyperglycemia + ketosis + acidosis. Treatment: IV fluids (1-2L NS first hour), IV insulin drip, potassium replacement (add K when <5.3 and urinating), search for precipitant. ICU admission.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of DKA. What's the treatment priority order?
feedback.incorrect: DKA triad: Hyperglycemia + Anion gap acidosis + Ketones. Treatment order: FLUIDS first (1-2L NS), then insulin drip, then K+ (critical to monitor - drops rapidly with insulin). Always search for precipitant: infection, MI, medication noncompliance.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Uncomplicated hyperglycemia = metformin, outpatient management. DKA = life-threatening emergency, IV fluids + insulin + K+ monitoring + ICU. The acidosis and ketones make all the difference.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if this was a thin 19-year-old with rapid onset?
whatIfScenarios.description: Young, thin patient with 2-week history of symptoms and significant weight loss

chiefComplaint.original: I've been so thirsty and peeing all the time
chiefComplaint.modified: 19-year-old: I've lost 15 pounds in 2 weeks, I'm peeing constantly, and I'm exhausted

feedback.correct: Young + thin + rapid onset = Type 1 diabetes. NEEDS INSULIN (not oral agents). Check for DKA. Consider C-peptide and autoantibodies (GAD65, IA-2) to confirm. Endocrine referral for insulin education.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of Type 1 pattern. What's the critical treatment difference?
feedback.incorrect: Type 1 vs Type 2: Type 1 is autoimmune destruction of beta cells (young, thin, rapid onset, needs INSULIN). Type 2 is insulin resistance (older, obese, gradual onset, can start with oral agents). Type 1 REQUIRES insulin from diagnosis - will develop DKA without it.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Type 2 = insulin resistance, can try lifestyle + metformin first. Type 1 = no insulin production, MUST have insulin from day 1. Misdiagnosis is dangerous - Type 1 without insulin → DKA.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if there was new jaundice and abdominal pain?
whatIfScenarios.description: New diabetes in 62-year-old with recent weight loss, abdominal pain, and jaundice

chiefComplaint.original: I've been so thirsty and peeing all the time
chiefComplaint.modified: 62-year-old: I've lost 20 pounds, my blood sugar is suddenly high, and now my eyes are yellow and my belly hurts

feedback.correct: New-onset diabetes + weight loss + jaundice in older adult = pancreatic cancer until proven otherwise. Order CT abdomen with contrast. This is NOT routine Type 2 DM.
feedback.partial: Good concern for malignancy. What imaging is needed?
feedback.incorrect: NEW-ONSET diabetes after age 50 with weight loss, abdominal pain, or jaundice = red flag for pancreatic cancer. Up to 1% of new diabetes in older adults is due to pancreatic cancer. Needs CT imaging, not just diabetes management.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Typical Type 2 = gradual onset, obesity, family history, responds to treatment. New diabetes in older adult with weight loss/jaundice/pain = investigate for malignancy before assuming garden-variety diabetes.

female-28-prolactinoma.name: M. Torres
female-28-prolactinoma.chiefComplaint: I haven't had my period in 4 months and I'm having weird discharge from my nipples

missedConsequences.Pituitary Adenoma (Prolactinoma): Assumed to be 'stress-related' amenorrhea. Tumor grows, compresses optic chiasm, patient presents 2 years later with permanent visual loss.
missedConsequences.Pregnancy: Pregnancy test not done because patient 'doesn't think she's pregnant.' Actually pregnant, misses early prenatal care.
missedConsequences.Ectopic Pregnancy: If pregnant with abnormal symptoms, could miss ectopic with catastrophic consequences.

commonPitfalls: Not getting pregnancy test FIRST - must rule out pregnancy before any other workup
Attributing amenorrhea to 'stress' without checking prolactin level
Missing the visual field defects on exam - pituitary tumors compress the optic chiasm
Not asking about galactorrhea - patients may not volunteer this symptom due to embarrassment
Forgetting medications that cause hyperprolactinemia (antipsychotics, metoclopramide)
Missing that hypothyroidism can elevate prolactin (elevated TRH)

mcqs.question: A 28-year-old woman has amenorrhea and bilateral milky nipple discharge. What test should you order FIRST?

options.text: Prolactin level - the galactorrhea indicates hyperprolactinemia
options.explanation: While prolactin is important, pregnancy must be excluded FIRST.

options.text: MRI of pituitary - to look for tumor
options.explanation: MRI may be needed but is not the first test.

options.text: Pregnancy test - must exclude pregnancy before other workup
options.explanation: ALWAYS pregnancy test first in amenorrhea. Pregnancy is the most common cause of secondary amenorrhea. Can't interpret other tests properly if pregnancy isn't excluded.

options.text: TSH - thyroid disease can cause amenorrhea
options.explanation: TSH is important but pregnancy test comes first.

mcqs.question: On visual field testing, you find she cannot see objects in the temporal visual fields of both eyes (bitemporal hemianopsia). What does this localize?

options.text: Optic nerve lesion
options.explanation: Optic nerve lesion causes monocular vision loss, not bitemporal pattern.

options.text: Optic chiasm compression (likely pituitary tumor)
options.explanation: BITEMPORAL HEMIANOPSIA = optic chiasm lesion. The optic chiasm sits just above the pituitary. A pituitary tumor growing upward compresses the crossing fibers, causing loss of TEMPORAL fields in BOTH eyes.

options.text: Occipital lobe stroke
options.explanation: Occipital lesion causes homonymous hemianopsia (same side of both eyes), not bitemporal.

options.text: Retinal detachment
options.explanation: Retinal detachment is unilateral and doesn't cause this pattern.

allDiagnoses.name: Hyperprolactinemia (Prolactinoma)
allDiagnoses.rationale: ⭐ ACTUAL DIAGNOSIS. Amenorrhea + galactorrhea + headache + visual changes = pituitary tumor until proven otherwise.

allDiagnoses.name: Pregnancy
allDiagnoses.rationale: Most common cause of secondary amenorrhea. Must rule out first even if patient doubts it.

allDiagnoses.name: Hypothyroidism
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can cause amenorrhea and elevated prolactin. TRH stimulates prolactin release.

allDiagnoses.name: Pituitary Tumor (any type)
allDiagnoses.rationale: Must consider given galactorrhea and visual symptoms. Could be prolactinoma or other sellar mass.

allDiagnoses.name: Ectopic Pregnancy
allDiagnoses.rationale: If pregnancy test is positive, must evaluate for ectopic.

allDiagnoses.name: Polycystic Ovary Syndrome (PCOS)
allDiagnoses.rationale: Causes irregular periods but typically with hyperandrogenism (hirsutism, acne), not galactorrhea.

allDiagnoses.name: Hypothalamic Amenorrhea
allDiagnoses.rationale: From stress, exercise, low weight. Wouldn't cause galactorrhea or visual symptoms.

allDiagnoses.name: Medication-Induced
allDiagnoses.rationale: Antipsychotics, metoclopramide can elevate prolactin. Patient not on these medications.

female-28-prolactinoma.oldcartsNarrative: "I haven't had my period in about 4 months. Before that, my cycles were pretty regular - every 28-30 days. I'm not pregnant - at least I don't think I am. I've been using condoms with my boyfriend. The weird thing is I've noticed some milky discharge from both nipples when I squeeze them. I also get some discharge on my bras. I've been having headaches more often lately, maybe twice a week, and sometimes things look a little blurry at the edges of my vision. I'm not on any birth control pills. I don't take any regular medications. No, I'm not under more stress than usual, and my weight has been stable. I'm not exercising excessively. I do feel more tired than usual."

historyQuestions.text: When did you notice your periods stop?
historyQuestions.answer: About 4 months ago. At first I thought I was just late, but then another month passed and still nothing. That's when I started getting worried.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Timeline establishes secondary amenorrhea; 4 months duration requires workup

historyQuestions.text: When was your last normal menstrual period?
historyQuestions.answer: About 4 months ago. Before that, my cycles were regular - every 28-30 days, lasting about 5 days.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Establishing secondary amenorrhea (previously normal cycles now absent). Must rule out pregnancy.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about the nipple discharge.
historyQuestions.answer: It's milky white, comes from both breasts when I squeeze them. Sometimes I notice spots on my bra. It's been happening for about 3 months.
historyQuestions.reasoning: BILATERAL MILKY DISCHARGE (galactorrhea) = hyperprolactinemia until proven otherwise. Must check prolactin level.

historyQuestions.text: You mentioned vision changes - describe those.
historyQuestions.answer: Sometimes I don't see things on the sides of my vision. Like I'll be walking and not notice someone approaching from the side. It's subtle but I've noticed it.
historyQuestions.reasoning: VISUAL FIELD CHANGES are red flag for pituitary tumor compressing optic chiasm. Must do visual field testing.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about the headaches.
historyQuestions.answer: They're usually behind my eyes or at my temples. Dull, achy. Maybe twice a week. Tylenol helps. They started around when my periods stopped.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Headaches can occur with pituitary tumors from mass effect or stretching of dura. Temporal correlation with amenorrhea is concerning.

historyQuestions.text: Is there any chance you could be pregnant?
historyQuestions.answer: I don't think so. My boyfriend and I use condoms. I haven't had any morning sickness or breast tenderness other than the discharge.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Must always ask directly. Patients may not think they're pregnant. Condoms can fail. MUST test.

historyQuestions.text: How old were you when you got your first period?
historyQuestions.answer: I was 12. Everything was normal - I developed at about the same time as my friends.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Establishing normal puberty and menarche rules out primary amenorrhea and developmental issues

historyQuestions.text: Before this, what were your periods like - flow, duration, pain?
historyQuestions.answer: Pretty normal. 5 days of bleeding, moderate flow, some cramping but nothing terrible. I never needed to see a doctor about them.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Baseline menstrual characteristics help establish this as secondary amenorrhea with prior normal cycles

historyQuestions.text: Have you ever been pregnant?
historyQuestions.answer: No, never. No miscarriages or abortions.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Obstetric history (G0P0) important for fertility counseling and ruling out postpartum causes

historyQuestions.text: What birth control methods have you used?
historyQuestions.answer: I was on the pill from 18 to 25. Stopped 3 years ago because we were thinking about having kids eventually. Now just condoms.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Contraceptive history; post-pill amenorrhea wouldn't persist 3 years later

historyQuestions.text: Are you sexually active?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, with my boyfriend. We've been together for 2 years.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Confirms sexual activity and pregnancy must be ruled out

historyQuestions.text: Are you taking any medications, including psychiatric medications?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I don't take any medications regularly. Just Tylenol for headaches sometimes.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Antipsychotics and metoclopramide are common causes of hyperprolactinemia. Ruling out medication cause.

historyQuestions.text: Have you noticed any cold intolerance, constipation, dry skin, or weight gain?
historyQuestions.answer: No, nothing like that. My weight has been stable, no constipation, I feel cold normally.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Screening for hypothyroidism which can cause hyperprolactinemia. Symptoms absent here.

historyQuestions.text: Any increased facial hair, acne, or hair thinning?
historyQuestions.answer: No, nothing like that. My skin has actually been pretty clear.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Absence of hyperandrogenic symptoms argues against PCOS

historyQuestions.text: Any significant weight changes or intense exercise?
historyQuestions.answer: My weight has been stable. I exercise moderately - yoga and some running, maybe 3-4 times a week. Nothing extreme.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Rules out hypothalamic amenorrhea from energy deficit or overexercise

historyQuestions.text: Any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Important before any treatment including potential surgery or medications

historyQuestions.text: Any other medical problems?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I've been healthy. Never had any major illnesses or hospitalizations.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Confirms previously healthy patient with new symptoms

historyQuestions.text: Any prior surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: I had my wisdom teeth out in college. Nothing else.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No prior head/pituitary surgery

historyQuestions.text: Any family history of pituitary problems or hormonal issues?
historyQuestions.answer: Not that I know of. My mom had thyroid problems but nothing with the pituitary.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Some pituitary tumors can be familial (MEN1 syndrome)

historyQuestions.text: Are you trying to get pregnant or concerned about fertility?
historyQuestions.answer: Not right now, but my boyfriend and I have talked about having kids in the next couple years. Is this going to affect that?
historyQuestions.reasoning: Important for counseling - prolactinomas are treatable and fertility can often be restored

historyQuestions.text: Any changes in your sex drive?
historyQuestions.answer: Now that you mention it, I haven't been as interested lately. I thought it was just stress.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Decreased libido is common with hyperprolactinemia

historyQuestions.text: How's your energy level?
historyQuestions.answer: I've been more tired than usual. I thought it was just from working a lot.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Fatigue can occur with pituitary dysfunction affecting multiple hormones

social_stress_level.answer: Work has been really stressful. I've been up for promotion. Lots of pressure.
social_stress_level.relevance: Stress can affect menstrual cycles through hypothalamic-pituitary axis, but organic cause must be ruled out first.

exercise_activity_level.answer: I do yoga a few times a week, some running. Not extreme - maybe 3-4 hours total per week.
exercise_activity_level.relevance: Moderate exercise - not excessive enough to explain hypothalamic amenorrhea.

actualDiagnosis.name: Pituitary Macroadenoma (Prolactinoma) with Optic Chiasm Compression
keyFindings: BITEMPORAL HEMIANOPSIA on visual field testing
MRI: 1.8 cm pituitary macroadenoma with suprasellar extension
teachingPoints: PREGNANCY FIRST: Always rule out pregnancy first in amenorrhea workup. It's the most common cause of secondary amenorrhea.
GALACTORRHEA + AMENORRHEA = CHECK PROLACTIN: This combination is classic for hyperprolactinemia.
VISUAL FIELDS ARE CRITICAL: Pituitary tumors grow upward and compress the optic chiasm. BITEMPORAL HEMIANOPSIA is the classic finding.
PROLACTIN LEVEL CORRELATES WITH TUMOR SIZE: Prolactin >200 suggests macroprolactinoma. Hook effect can cause falsely normal prolactin with giant tumors.
DOPAMINE AGONISTS ARE FIRST-LINE: Cabergoline or bromocriptine shrink prolactinomas and normalize prolactin in 80-90% of cases. Surgery is rarely needed.
ASK ABOUT GALACTORRHEA: Patients may be embarrassed and not volunteer this symptom. Ask directly.
CHECK THYROID: Hypothyroidism can cause hyperprolactinemia (TRH stimulates prolactin). Always check TSH.

closureScript.diagnosis: Ms. Torres, we've found the cause of your missed periods and the nipple discharge. You have a benign tumor in your pituitary gland - a small gland at the base of your brain. This type of tumor is called a prolactinoma, and it's producing too much of a hormone called prolactin, which is why you're having the symptoms you described. The tumor is also pressing on the nerves for your vision, which explains why you've been having trouble seeing things to the sides.
closureScript.plan: The good news is that this type of tumor usually responds very well to medication - we don't need surgery. I'm starting you on a medication called cabergoline that will shrink the tumor and bring your prolactin level back to normal. Most women see their periods return and the discharge stop within a few months. We'll repeat the MRI in 3 months to make sure the tumor is shrinking, and we'll have ophthalmology follow your vision.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: Call us if you have a sudden severe headache, sudden vision changes, or nausea and vomiting - these could indicate a problem with the tumor that needs urgent attention.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: I know hearing you have a 'brain tumor' is scary, but I want to reassure you - prolactinomas are benign, meaning they're not cancer. They don't spread, and they respond beautifully to medication in most cases. Your vision should improve as the tumor shrinks. And yes, you should be able to have children in the future once this is controlled.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the pregnancy test was positive?

chiefComplaint.original: I haven't had my period in 4 months
chiefComplaint.modified: I haven't had my period in 4 months. I just found out my pregnancy test is positive.

feedback.correct: PREGNANCY is the #1 cause of secondary amenorrhea. Always test first before any other workup. Now need: quantitative hCG, ultrasound for dating/location/viability, prenatal care initiation.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of pregnancy as cause. What immediate steps are needed?
feedback.incorrect: ALWAYS rule out pregnancy FIRST in any woman with amenorrhea. Most common cause of secondary amenorrhea. Once confirmed, focus shifts to: confirming intrauterine location (rule out ectopic), dating, viability, and initiating prenatal care.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Entire amenorrhea workup (prolactin, TSH, FSH) is unnecessary if pregnancy is the cause. Always test pregnancy first - it's cheap, fast, and the most common answer.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if there was hirsutism and obesity?
whatIfScenarios.description: Amenorrhea with acne, hirsutism, obesity, and irregular periods since menarche

chiefComplaint.original: I haven't had my period in 4 months
chiefComplaint.modified: My periods have always been irregular - sometimes I go 3-4 months. I also have a lot of facial hair and acne.

feedback.correct: Rotterdam criteria for PCOS: 2 of 3 - oligo/anovulation, hyperandrogenism (clinical or biochemical), polycystic ovaries on US. Screen for metabolic syndrome (glucose, lipids). Treatment: lifestyle, OCPs, metformin.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of PCOS. What are the diagnostic criteria?
feedback.incorrect: PCOS is most common cause of anovulatory infertility. Rotterdam criteria: need 2 of 3 - oligo/anovulation, hyperandrogenism, polycystic ovaries. Associated with insulin resistance, metabolic syndrome, T2DM, NASH, endometrial cancer. Lifestyle modification is key.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Prolactinoma = elevated prolactin, galactorrhea. PCOS = hyperandrogenism (acne, hirsutism), irregular periods since puberty, metabolic features. Different hormonal patterns, different management.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if patient was an underweight athlete?
whatIfScenarios.description: Amenorrhea in thin long-distance runner with stress fractures

chiefComplaint.original: I haven't had my period in 4 months
chiefComplaint.modified: I'm a competitive runner. I haven't had my period in 8 months. I've also had two stress fractures this year.

feedback.correct: Female Athlete Triad: low energy availability → amenorrhea → low bone density. RED-S is broader concept. Stress fractures are the tip of the iceberg. Treatment: increase caloric intake, decrease exercise, address underlying eating disorder if present.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of athletic amenorrhea. What's the triad?
feedback.incorrect: Female Athlete Triad: 1) Low energy availability (often disordered eating), 2) Menstrual dysfunction, 3) Low bone mineral density. Stress fractures are a major complication. Labs show low LH/FSH (hypothalamic suppression), low estrogen. Must address energy deficit.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: PCOS = overweight, hyperandrogenism, high LH:FSH ratio. Hypothalamic amenorrhea = underweight, low LH/FSH, low estrogen, energy deficit. Opposite ends of the spectrum.

allDiagnoses.name: Thyroid Storm
allDiagnoses.rationale: Graves' disease off medication + iodine contrast + hyperthermia + AFib + agitation = thyroid storm.

allDiagnoses.name: Sepsis

allDiagnoses.name: Contrast Reaction

physicalExam.reasoning: CNS manifestations of severe thyrotoxicosis

labs.name: TSH, Free T4, Free T3
labs.result: TSH <0.01 mIU/L (suppressed), Free T4 5.8 ng/dL (very high, normal 0.8-1.8), Free T3 18 pg/mL (very high, normal 2.3-4.2). Confirms severe thyrotoxicosis.
labs.reasoning: Confirms thyrotoxicosis BUT DO NOT wait for results to treat. Thyroid storm is a CLINICAL diagnosis using Burch-Wartofsky score. Start treatment immediately.

labs.name: CBC
labs.result: WBC 14.2 (mild leukocytosis), Hgb 12.8, Plt 198. Leukocytosis common in thyroid storm without infection.
labs.reasoning: Mild leukocytosis is common in thyroid storm. Also rules out severe infection as cause of fever.

labs.name: CMP
labs.result: Na 138, K 3.8, Cr 1.1, Glucose 168, AST 68, ALT 54, Alk Phos 142. Mild liver enzyme elevation.
labs.reasoning: Elevated LFTs common in thyroid storm. Hyperglycemia from catecholamine surge. Baseline for monitoring.

labs.name: PT/INR
labs.reasoning: Baseline before anticoagulation if indicated for AFib.

labs.name: Blood Cultures x 2
labs.reasoning: Must rule out infection as precipitant. Fever could be from sepsis or thyroid storm - often can't distinguish initially.

labs.name: Lactate
labs.result: Lactate 1.8 mmol/L (mildly elevated). Reflects hypermetabolic state.
labs.reasoning: Mild elevation expected from hypermetabolic state. Would be concerning if very high - suggests tissue hypoperfusion.

labs.name: Random Cortisol
labs.reasoning: Relative adrenal insufficiency can occur in thyroid storm due to rapid cortisol consumption. Supports giving stress-dose steroids.

labs.name: TSI (Thyroid Stimulating Immunoglobulin)
labs.reasoning: Confirms autoimmune etiology (Graves') but not urgent for acute management. Result takes days.

imaging.name: 12-lead ECG
imaging.result: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response, rate 148 bpm. No acute ischemic changes.
imaging.reasoning: Documents AFib with RVR - common in thyroid storm. Need rate control. Also rules out STEMI.

imaging.name: Chest X-ray
imaging.result: Mild pulmonary vascular congestion. No infiltrate. Cardiomegaly borderline.
imaging.reasoning: Assess for pulmonary edema (high-output heart failure) and rule out pneumonia as precipitant.

imaging.name: CT Head (if persistent AMS)
imaging.reasoning: Consider if altered mental status doesn't improve with treatment, to rule out other causes.

procedures.name: Central Venous Access
procedures.result: Right IJ central line placed for medication administration and CVP monitoring.
procedures.reasoning: May need for vasopressors if develops thyroid storm-related shock. Also for multiple IV infusions.

procedures.name: Arterial Line
procedures.result: Radial arterial line for continuous BP monitoring.
procedures.reasoning: ICU-level hemodynamic monitoring. Useful if patient becomes unstable.

procedures.name: Foley Catheter
procedures.reasoning: Monitor urine output as marker of perfusion in critically ill patient.

procedures.name: Active Cooling Measures
procedures.result: Cooling blanket applied. Acetaminophen 1g IV given.
procedures.reasoning: Hyperthermia must be treated aggressively. Avoid aspirin (displaces T4 from binding proteins, worsening toxicity).

treatments.name: PTU 200mg PO/NG q4h (or Methimazole 20mg q4h)
treatments.result: PTU 200mg loading dose given, then 200mg q4h via NG tube. PTU preferred over methimazole in storm.
treatments.reasoning: GIVE FIRST before iodine. PTU blocks thyroid hormone synthesis AND peripheral T4→T3 conversion. Methimazole is alternative but doesn't block peripheral conversion.

treatments.name: Iodine (SSKI or Lugol's) - GIVE 1 HOUR AFTER PTU
treatments.result: SSKI 5 drops PO q6h started 1 hour after PTU loading dose.
treatments.reasoning: TIMING CRITICAL: Must give iodine at least 1 hour AFTER thionamide (PTU/methimazole). Otherwise, iodine will fuel more hormone synthesis (Jod-Basedow phenomenon). Iodine blocks hormone release via Wolff-Chaikoff effect.

treatments.name: Propranolol 60-80mg PO q4h (or IV esmolol)
treatments.result: Propranolol 60mg q4h via NG tube. Alternative: esmolol drip if unable to take PO.
treatments.reasoning: Beta-blockade is CRITICAL for symptom control. Propranolol preferred because it also inhibits peripheral T4→T3 conversion. Targets HR <100. Careful if patient in heart failure.

treatments.name: Hydrocortisone 100mg IV q8h (or Dexamethasone 2mg IV q6h)
treatments.result: Hydrocortisone 100mg IV q8h started.
treatments.reasoning: Stress-dose steroids are standard in thyroid storm. Blocks peripheral T4→T3 conversion. Also treats relative adrenal insufficiency that can occur.

treatments.name: Anticoagulation for AFib (if no contraindication)
treatments.result: Heparin drip initiated after confirming no contraindications. Will transition to warfarin for long-term.
treatments.reasoning: New AFib in thyroid storm is often self-limiting once euthyroid, but anticoagulation reduces stroke risk while in AFib.

treatments.name: Aggressive IV Fluids
treatments.result: LR bolus 2L, then 150 mL/hr. Monitor for fluid overload.
treatments.reasoning: Patients are often volume depleted from fever, diaphoresis, and vomiting. Replace aggressively but watch for high-output heart failure.

treatments.name: Acetaminophen for Fever (NOT aspirin)
treatments.result: Acetaminophen 1g IV q6h PRN fever.
treatments.reasoning: Aspirin is CONTRAINDICATED - it displaces thyroid hormone from binding proteins, increasing free hormone and worsening toxicity. Use acetaminophen only.

treatments.name: Cholestyramine (adjunct)
treatments.result: Cholestyramine 4g PO q6h added as adjunctive therapy.
treatments.reasoning: Binds thyroid hormone in gut, reducing enterohepatic recirculation. Adjunctive therapy in severe storm.

consults.name: ICU Admission
consults.result: Admitted to medical ICU for continuous monitoring and aggressive management.
consults.reasoning: Thyroid storm has 10-30% mortality. Requires ICU-level monitoring, multiple IV drips, and close hemodynamic surveillance.

consults.name: Endocrinology (STAT)
consults.result: Endocrinology consulted emergently. Agrees with treatment regimen. Will follow for long-term thyroid management.
consults.reasoning: Endocrinology expertise essential for managing thyroid storm and planning long-term definitive therapy (radioactive iodine or thyroidectomy).

consults.name: Cardiology (for AFib/heart failure)
consults.result: Cardiology aware for rate control optimization and anticoagulation guidance.
consults.reasoning: New AFib with RVR and possible high-output heart failure may benefit from cardiology input.

disposition.name: Medical ICU Admission
disposition.result: Admitted to MICU. Continuous telemetry, arterial line, central line. Strict I/Os. Neuro checks q2h.
disposition.reasoning: Thyroid storm is a medical emergency. ICU required for continuous monitoring, multiple drips, and rapid intervention if decompensates.

disposition.name: Family Communication
disposition.result: Family updated on critical nature of condition. Discussed that thyroid storm can be fatal but prognosis good with aggressive treatment.
disposition.reasoning: Family needs to understand severity. Prepare for possibility of worsening before improving.

actualDiagnosis.name: Thyroid Storm (Iodine-Induced)
keyFindings: Graves' disease - stopped methimazole months ago
Received iodinated contrast for CT scan
teachingPoints: Thyroid storm is a medical emergency with 10-30% mortality
Burch-Wartofsky score helps diagnose (>45 = storm)
Iodine contrast can precipitate storm in hyperthyroid patients
Other precipitants: surgery, infection, trauma, DKA
Treatment order matters: PTU or methimazole FIRST, then iodine
Beta-blockers control symptoms (propranolol also blocks T4→T3)
Stress-dose steroids are part of treatment
Check thyroid status before contrast in patients with goiter/thyroid history

closureScript.diagnosis: Your mother is having a thyroid storm - a severe, life-threatening surge of thyroid hormone. The contrast dye from her CT scan triggered this because her Graves' disease wasn't being treated.
closureScript.plan: We're starting multiple medications to block her thyroid immediately. She needs to be in the ICU. This is a medical emergency that requires aggressive treatment.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: Thyroid storm can be fatal if not treated quickly. We're doing everything we can. Once she's stable, she absolutely cannot stop her thyroid medication again.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: I know this is terrifying. The good news is we recognized what's happening and are treating it aggressively. Most patients do survive thyroid storm with prompt treatment.

actualDiagnosis.name: Female Athlete Triad (Relative Energy Deficiency in Sport - RED-S)
keyFindings: Endurance athlete with high training volume (70-80 miles/week)
Significant weight loss with caloric restriction
Recurrent stress fractures (2 in one year)
Low BMI with signs of malnutrition
teachingPoints: Female Athlete Triad = Low Energy Availability + Menstrual Dysfunction + Low Bone Mineral Density
Amenorrhea in athletes is NOT normal - it's a sign of hypothalamic suppression from energy deficit
Now called RED-S (Relative Energy Deficiency in Sport) to include males and broader health effects
The root cause is ENERGY DEFICIT - intake not matching expenditure
Low estrogen leads to bone loss that may be irreversible if prolonged
Stress fractures are a late sign - bone damage has already occurred
Treatment: Increase energy intake, decrease training, multidisciplinary team (MD, dietitian, psychologist)
Cannot return to sport until energy balance restored and menses return
Coaching comments about weight are a major risk factor - should be reported

closureScript.diagnosis: You have what we call the Female Athlete Triad - it's a combination of not eating enough for your training level, losing your period, and developing weak bones. That's why you're getting stress fractures. Your body is in survival mode, shutting down non-essential functions like reproduction because it doesn't have enough fuel.
closureScript.plan: The most important thing is to eat more - significantly more. You need to work with our sports dietitian to develop a nutrition plan. You cannot run right now - your bones are too fragile and you'll just get more fractures. You'll also meet with our sports psychologist because the relationship with food and body image is an important part of this.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: I have to be very direct with you: if this continues, you may cause permanent damage to your bones and your ability to have children. Some of the bone loss you've already had may never come back. Your running career is actually in MORE danger if you keep going like this than if you stop and recover properly.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: I know your coach is telling you to lose weight, but that advice is medically dangerous for you. I'm going to speak with athletic training staff about this. Your health has to come first. The strongest, fastest version of you is one that's properly fueled. Many elite athletes have come back from the Triad stronger than before - but only by addressing it, not by ignoring it.

new-onset-t1dm.name: Marcus J.
new-onset-t1dm.variantDescription: New-onset Type 1 DM presenting with DKA - from ICU to diagnosis to lifestyle education

longitudinalDays.title: Hour 0: Emergency Department
longitudinalDays.presentation: 19-year-old previously healthy male brought in by roommate. Found confused in dorm room, vomited multiple times. Roommate says he's been drinking lots of water and using the bathroom constantly for 2-3 weeks. Lost about 15 pounds. No medical history.

historyQuestions.text: When did you start feeling sick?
historyQuestions.answer: (Confused, slow to respond) I don't know... few weeks... really thirsty...
historyQuestions.reasoning: Polyuria, polydipsia for weeks suggests undiagnosed diabetes with acute decompensation

historyQuestions.text: (To roommate) What have you noticed?
historyQuestions.answer: He's been peeing like every hour, drinking gallons of water, lost a ton of weight. I thought it was stress. Today he was really out of it and kept throwing up.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Classic triad: polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss = new diabetes until proven otherwise

historyQuestions.text: How much weight have you lost?
historyQuestions.answer: (Roommate) He was 180 at start of semester, now he looks way skinnier. Maybe 160?
historyQuestions.reasoning: 20 lb weight loss over weeks indicates severe catabolism from insulin deficiency

historyQuestions.text: Does your stomach hurt?
historyQuestions.answer: Yeah... really bad... (holds abdomen)
historyQuestions.reasoning: Abdominal pain is common in DKA - can mimic surgical abdomen. Usually resolves with treatment.

historyQuestions.text: (To roommate) Any family history of diabetes?
historyQuestions.answer: I think his mom has diabetes? Not sure what type.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Family history increases risk. Type 1 has genetic component.

historyQuestions.text: Has he been sick recently? Infections?
historyQuestions.answer: He had a cold maybe 3-4 weeks ago. Nothing major.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Viral illness can precipitate DKA in new-onset T1DM or unmask undiagnosed diabetes

historyQuestions.text: Any medications, drugs, alcohol?
historyQuestions.answer: No medications. Drinks beer on weekends but nothing crazy. No drugs.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Rules out medication-induced causes. SGLT2 inhibitors can cause euglycemic DKA.

historyQuestions.text: Any allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: (Roommate) None that I know of.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Important before starting insulin and other medications

physicalExam.finding: Thin young man, appears acutely ill. Lethargic but arousable. Dry mucous membranes. Fruity odor on breath.
physicalExam.reasoning: FRUITY BREATH = ketones. Lethargy indicates severe DKA. Dry membranes = dehydration.

physicalExam.finding: Tachypneic with deep, rapid respirations (Kussmaul breathing). Lungs clear bilaterally.
physicalExam.reasoning: KUSSMAUL BREATHING is respiratory compensation for metabolic acidosis - body blowing off CO2

physicalExam.finding: Tachycardic, regular rhythm. Weak peripheral pulses. Delayed capillary refill (4 seconds).
physicalExam.reasoning: Signs of hypovolemia/dehydration. DKA causes massive fluid losses (osmotic diuresis).

physicalExam.finding: Diffusely tender, no rebound or guarding. Hypoactive bowel sounds. No organomegaly.
physicalExam.reasoning: DKA causes abdominal pain - can mimic acute abdomen. No peritoneal signs = likely metabolic, not surgical.

physicalExam.finding: GCS 13 (E3V4M6). Oriented to person only. Moving all extremities. No focal deficits.
physicalExam.reasoning: Altered mental status indicates severe DKA. Need to monitor for cerebral edema (rare but fatal).

physicalExam.finding: Dry, poor skin turgor. Tenting present. No rashes or lesions.
physicalExam.reasoning: Skin turgor confirms significant dehydration (5-10% body weight fluid deficit typical in DKA)

labInterpretation.dkaConfirmation: DKA CONFIRMED: Glucose >250, pH <7.3, HCO3 <18, AG >12, ketones positive

labs.name: Hemoglobin A1c
labs.reasoning: HbA1c >6.5% confirms diabetes. Very high A1c suggests this has been brewing for months undiagnosed.

labs.name: C-peptide level
labs.result: 0.2 ng/mL (low) - suggests minimal endogenous insulin production
labs.reasoning: Low C-peptide confirms Type 1 (insulin-deficient) vs Type 2. Important for long-term management.

labs.name: GAD65 antibodies
labs.result: Positive (>250 U/mL) - confirms autoimmune Type 1 diabetes
labs.reasoning: Autoantibodies confirm autoimmune destruction of beta cells = Type 1 DM

labs.name: Lipase
labs.reasoning: Can be elevated in DKA without pancreatitis. If >3x upper limit with persistent pain, consider pancreatitis.

labs.name: Urinalysis
labs.result: Glucose 4+, Ketones 4+, No WBCs or bacteria
labs.reasoning: Confirms glucosuria and ketonuria. No UTI as precipitant.

labs.name: Blood cultures
labs.reasoning: Rule out occult infection as DKA precipitant. WBC often elevated in DKA even without infection.

labs.name: ECG
labs.reasoning: CRITICAL: Monitor for hyperkalemia. Peaked T waves are early sign. K will DROP rapidly with insulin - need to monitor.

treatments.name: IV Normal Saline 1L bolus, then 500mL/hr
treatments.result: Fluid resuscitation initiated. Plan 4-6L in first 24 hours.
treatments.reasoning: FLUIDS FIRST! Average deficit is 5-10L. NS initially, then can switch to 1/2 NS when stable.

treatments.name: Regular insulin drip 0.1 units/kg/hr
treatments.result: Started at 7 units/hour (weight 70 kg). Check glucose hourly.
treatments.reasoning: Start insulin AFTER first liter of fluid AND confirm K >3.3. Insulin drives K into cells!

treatments.name: Potassium replacement protocol
treatments.result: K 5.8 now - hold K replacement. Recheck in 2 hours. Add 20-40 mEq/L to fluids when K <5.0
treatments.reasoning: POTASSIUM MANAGEMENT IS CRITICAL. K appears high but total body K is depleted. Will crash with insulin. Replace when <5.0.

treatments.name: Add D5 to fluids when glucose <250
treatments.result: Protocol: When glucose <250, add D5 to IVF. Continue insulin until gap closes.
treatments.reasoning: Don't stop insulin when glucose normalizes! Continue until anion gap closes. Add dextrose to prevent hypoglycemia.

treatments.name: Sodium bicarbonate
treatments.result: NOT INDICATED - pH 7.18 is above threshold
treatments.reasoning: Bicarb only if pH <6.9. Otherwise harmful - shifts K, may worsen CNS acidosis, impairs O2 delivery.

treatments.name: Phosphate replacement
treatments.result: Check phosphate level. Replace if <1.0 mg/dL.
treatments.reasoning: Phosphate often depleted. Severe hypophosphatemia can cause weakness, rhabdomyolysis.

consults.name: ICU admission
consults.result: Admit to ICU for DKA protocol and hourly monitoring.
consults.reasoning: Severe DKA (pH <7.2) requires ICU for close monitoring of glucose, K, pH, mental status.

consults.name: Endocrinology consult
consults.result: Consult placed for new-onset Type 1 DM education and management.
consults.reasoning: New diagnosis of T1DM requires extensive education, insulin teaching, and outpatient follow-up planning.

decisionPoint.question: You've confirmed DKA in a new-onset diabetic. What is your FIRST action?

options.title: Start IV fluids immediately, then insulin
options.description: 1L NS bolus now, start insulin drip after first liter and K >3.3

feedback.message: CORRECT! Fluids first, then insulin. Giving insulin before fluids can cause cardiovascular collapse. Also must confirm K >3.3 before insulin (insulin drives K into cells).
DANGEROUS! Insulin before adequate fluid resuscitation can cause cardiovascular collapse. Insulin also drives K into cells - if K <3.3, can cause fatal arrhythmia. FLUIDS FIRST!

options.title: Give sodium bicarbonate for acidosis
options.description: pH 7.18 is dangerously low - correct the acidosis

feedback.message: Bicarbonate is only indicated if pH <6.9. Above that threshold, bicarb can worsen intracellular acidosis, shift K dangerously, and impair oxygen delivery. The acidosis will correct with fluids and insulin.

teachingPoints: DKA DIAGNOSTIC CRITERIA: Glucose >250, pH <7.3, HCO3 <18, Anion gap >12, Ketones positive
FLUIDS BEFORE INSULIN: Give 1-2L NS before starting insulin. Insulin without volume can cause collapse.
POTASSIUM PARADOX: Serum K appears normal/high but total body K is depleted. K will crash with insulin. Replace when <5.0.
CORRECT SODIUM FOR GLUCOSE: Add 1.6 mEq/L to measured Na for every 100 mg/dL glucose above 100.

longitudinalDays.title: Hour 4: ICU Monitoring
longitudinalDays.nurseReport: Received 3L NS so far. On insulin drip at 7 units/hr. Urine output improving - 150mL in last hour. Still confused but more responsive. Glucose checks: 487→402→318. K dropped to 4.8 - started adding K to fluids.

decisionPoint.question: Glucose is 318 and dropping well. The anion gap is improving but still elevated at 22. What's your next step?

options.title: Continue current insulin drip, add D5 to fluids soon
options.description: Gap still open. Continue insulin until gap closes. Add dextrose when glucose <250.

feedback.message: CORRECT! The goal is to close the anion gap, not just lower glucose. Continue insulin drip and add D5 when glucose <250 to prevent hypoglycemia while continuing to clear ketones.

options.title: Stop insulin drip - glucose is coming down nicely

feedback.message: WRONG! Stopping insulin before the anion gap closes will cause ketosis to continue/worsen. The gap is still 22 (elevated). Continue insulin and add dextrose to fluids when glucose <250.

options.title: Decrease insulin drip rate
options.description: Glucose dropping too fast - slow it down

feedback.message: Rate of glucose drop (40-50 mg/dL/hr) is actually appropriate. Decreasing insulin will slow gap closure. Better approach: continue current rate and add D5 when glucose <250.

teachingPoints: ANION GAP IS THE GOAL: Don't stop insulin when glucose normalizes. Continue until AG <12.
ADD DEXTROSE, DON'T STOP INSULIN: When glucose <250, add D5 to fluids. This allows insulin to continue clearing ketones.
GLUCOSE DROP RATE: Target 50-75 mg/dL/hr. Faster drops risk cerebral edema (especially in children).
POTASSIUM MONITORING: Check K every 2-4 hours. Aggressive replacement needed as insulin drives K into cells.

longitudinalDays.title: Hour 12: Gap Closing
longitudinalDays.nurseReport: Much improved! Alert and oriented now. Eating lunch - first meal since admission. Anion gap closed. On D5 1/2NS with insulin drip. Glucose stable 150-180 range. K stable at 4.2 with replacement. Parents arrived and are at bedside.

decisionPoint.question: Anion gap is now 10 (closed), pH normalized, patient eating. Ready to transition off insulin drip?

options.title: Give subcutaneous long-acting insulin, then stop drip in 2 hours
options.description: Overlap SubQ and IV insulin before discontinuing drip

feedback.message: CORRECT! Must overlap subcutaneous and IV insulin by 1-2 hours. Long-acting insulin takes time to work. If you stop the drip without overlap, patient will go back into DKA.

options.title: Stop insulin drip now, start SubQ insulin with next meal
options.description: Gap is closed, can stop the drip

feedback.message: DANGEROUS! Type 1 diabetics have NO endogenous insulin. Stopping drip without SubQ overlap will cause rapid return to ketosis. Must overlap by 1-2 hours.

teachingPoints: OVERLAP IS ESSENTIAL: Give SubQ long-acting insulin 1-2 hours BEFORE stopping drip. Otherwise DKA will recur.
CRITERIA FOR DRIP DISCONTINUATION: AG closed, pH >7.3, HCO3 >18, patient eating, SubQ given with overlap.
NEW TYPE 1 INSULIN DOSING: Start ~0.5-0.6 units/kg/day total. Split 50% basal (long-acting) and 50% bolus (rapid with meals).
HONEYMOON PERIOD: New T1DM patients often have temporary partial beta cell recovery. Insulin needs may decrease before increasing.

longitudinalDays.title: Day 2: Diabetes Education
longitudinalDays.nurseReport: Transferred from ICU last night. On basal-bolus insulin regimen. Blood sugars ranging 140-220. Eating well. Diabetes educator and dietitian seeing him today. Parents very engaged.

educationTopics.notes: Explained autoimmune destruction of beta cells. Will need insulin forever.
Learning to use glucometer. Check before meals and bedtime minimum.
Practicing injection technique with saline. Will do supervised insulin injection at dinner.
How to prevent future DKA episodes

decisionPoint.question: Marcus is learning quickly but overwhelmed by all the information. What's the priority before discharge?

options.title: Hypoglycemia recognition/treatment AND sick day rules

feedback.message: CORRECT! Hypoglycemia and sick day rules are SURVIVAL SKILLS. Patient can learn fine-tuning later, but must know how to treat low blood sugar and prevent DKA recurrence before leaving.

options.title: Perfect carbohydrate counting
options.description: Need exact carb counting for good control

feedback.message: Carb counting is important for long-term control but can be refined over weeks/months with outpatient support. Safety skills (hypo treatment, sick day rules) are higher priority for discharge readiness.

teachingPoints: SURVIVAL SKILLS FOR DISCHARGE: 1) Insulin injection technique, 2) Glucose monitoring, 3) Hypoglycemia treatment, 4) Sick day rules, 5) When to call for help
RULE OF 15 FOR HYPOGLYCEMIA: If BG <70, eat 15g fast carbs, wait 15 min, recheck. Repeat if still low.
SICK DAY RULES: Never stop long-acting insulin. Check glucose and ketones frequently. Stay hydrated. Call if vomiting or ketones present.
INFORMATION OVERLOAD: New diabetics can only absorb so much. Focus on safety, refine management outpatient.

longitudinalDays.title: Day 3: Discharge
longitudinalDays.nurseReport: Ready for discharge! Successfully self-administered all insulin doses yesterday. Blood sugars 120-180 range. Demonstrated hypoglycemia treatment. Parents have glucagon kit. Follow-up appointments scheduled.

dischargeChecklist.item: Prescriptions sent: Lantus, Humalog, glucometer, test strips, ketone strips, glucagon

decisionPoint.question: Final discharge teaching. What's the most important message for this new Type 1 diabetic?

options.title: Never skip your long-acting insulin, even if not eating. Call us if vomiting or ketones positive.
options.description: Emphasize DKA prevention as the critical safety message

feedback.message: PERFECT! The #1 cause of recurrent DKA is insulin omission. Emphasizing that long-acting insulin is never optional and that early contact prevents crises is the key to keeping this patient out of the ICU.

options.title: Keep your blood sugars under 140 at all times

feedback.message: While good control is important long-term, setting unrealistic expectations for a brand-new diabetic can lead to frustration and dangerous hypoglycemia. Target A1c <7% is the goal, not perfection. Safety first, then optimization.

teachingPoints: DKA RECURRENCE PREVENTION: Most recurrent DKA is from insulin omission. Basal insulin is NEVER optional.
REALISTIC EXPECTATIONS: New diabetics will have imperfect control. Focus on safety and gradual improvement.
SUPPORT SYSTEMS: Connect with diabetes support groups, especially for young adults. Mental health support is important.
TECHNOLOGY OPTIONS: Discuss CGM and insulin pumps at follow-up when basics are mastered.

longitudinalDays.title: Week 2: Endocrinology Follow-up
longitudinalDays.patientUpdate: Marcus returns for his 2-week follow-up. He's been checking blood sugars 4 times daily, giving insulin appropriately. Had one mild hypoglycemic episode (BG 62) - treated correctly with juice. No further DKA symptoms. Back at school, roommate trained on glucagon. Adjusting to diagnosis emotionally with support from family and campus counseling.

decisionPoint.question: Marcus is doing well but had a high reading when he forgot his mealtime insulin. His current regimen is Lantus 18u QHS, Humalog 5u with meals. What adjustment?

options.title: Discuss CGM to help with awareness and patterns
options.description: Continuous glucose monitoring would help a busy college student

feedback.message: Excellent idea! CGM provides real-time glucose data, trend arrows, and alarms for highs and lows. Particularly helpful for young adults with variable schedules. Insurance coverage is improving for Type 1.

options.title: Increase Lantus to 22 units
options.description: Increase basal to bring down overall numbers

feedback.message: His fasting glucose is actually reasonable (142). Increasing basal significantly may cause overnight hypoglycemia. The high reading was from a missed bolus - a behavioral issue, not a basal dosing issue. CGM or reminder strategies would help more.

teachingPoints: CGM BENEFITS: Real-time glucose, trend arrows, alarms, pattern recognition. Game-changer for Type 1 management.
INSULIN PUMP OPTION: Can discuss once carb counting is mastered. Allows more flexible dosing.
HONEYMOON PERIOD: Insulin needs may decrease temporarily as remaining beta cells recover somewhat. Monitor for hypoglycemia.
PSYCHOSOCIAL SUPPORT: New diagnosis is emotionally challenging. Screen for diabetes distress and depression.

caseConclusion.summary: 19-year-old male presenting with new-onset Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus in DKA. Successfully treated with IV fluids, insulin drip, and electrolyte management. Transitioned to basal-bolus SubQ insulin with appropriate overlap. Completed diabetes survival skills education and discharged with close follow-up. Adjusting well at 2-week follow-up with plans for CGM initiation.
keyDecisions: Recognized DKA and initiated fluids BEFORE insulin
Monitored potassium closely and replaced appropriately
Continued insulin drip until anion gap closed, not just until glucose normalized
Overlapped SubQ and IV insulin before drip discontinuation
Prioritized safety skills (hypoglycemia, sick day rules) for discharge education
Connected patient with appropriate outpatient support
learningObjectives: Apply the DKA treatment protocol: fluids → insulin → potassium → monitor
Understand the importance of anion gap closure (not glucose) as treatment endpoint
Recognize the critical potassium management in DKA
Master the transition from IV to SubQ insulin with appropriate overlap
Identify survival skills for new Type 1 diabetics before discharge
Appreciate the psychosocial impact of a new diabetes diagnosis

actualDiagnosis.name: Diabetic Ketoacidosis - New-onset Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus
keyFindings: Classic presentation: polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, vomiting, confusion
Kussmaul breathing and fruity breath odor
Glucose 487, pH 7.18, HCO3 8, AG 28, ketones elevated
Low C-peptide and positive GAD65 antibodies confirming Type 1
Precipitant likely viral illness 3-4 weeks prior
teachingPoints: DKA is the presenting symptom in 25-30% of new Type 1 diabetics
FLUIDS BEFORE INSULIN - always
Potassium management is critical - appears high but total body depleted
Close the anion gap, not just the glucose
Overlap SubQ and IV insulin for safe transition
Survival skills before discharge: injection technique, hypo treatment, sick day rules

new-onset-t1dm.clinicalPearl: DKA is a 'whole-body potassium deficiency despite normal or high serum potassium.' The high serum K is because acidosis and insulin deficiency push K out of cells. Once you give insulin, K will crash. Check K every 2 hours and replace aggressively.
